findings.topic: The rota CANNOT be the source of truth for the actual trading day — prod data disproves it
findings.detail: Compared rota_shifts against special_hours in prod (tfcasgxopxegwrabvwat). They disagree in BOTH directions: 2026-04-03 (Good Friday) special_hours.opens=16:00 but earliest rota shift = 14:00 (rota 2h EARLIER); 2026-05-25 (Spring bank holiday) special_hours.opens=12:00 (note: 'Kitchen and bar open from 12pm!') but earliest rota shift = 16:00 (rota 4h LATER — nobody was rostered for the advertised opening). On normal weeks (2026-07-06 to 2026-07-26) every day's earliest scheduled shift start is IDENTICAL to business_hours.opens, except Sunday where a 10:30–11:30 'Cleaning' shift starts 90min BEFORE the 12:00 open, and Saturday 2026-07-11 / 2026-07-18 where max(end_time)=21:00 against a 22:00 close (no bar close shift rostered that day).
findings.implication: min(start_time)/max(end_time) over shifts is NOT 'the pub opened at X and closed at Y'. Pre-open cleaning shifts pull the earliest earlier; missing/short-rostered close shifts pull the latest earlier; and on the two most operationally unusual days of the sample the rota simply did not track the real hours. The premise 'the rota, not business_hours, is the source of truth for the trading day' is not supported by the data. Both are PLANS; neither records what actually happened.
findings.blocker: YES — this is the spec's load-bearing assumption and it is false as stated. Owner must choose: (a) derive the trading day from special_hours→business_hours (what the app already does, see below) and use the rota only for WHO, or (b) fund a new explicit source of actual open/close (e.g. the open checklist's own completion timestamp becomes the de facto 'we opened' record). Recommend (a)+(b): resolve the EXPECTED day from special_hours→business_hours, and let the checklist itself record the ACTUAL.

findings.topic: Something already reconciles trading hours — and it does not read the rota
findings.detail: src/app/api/foh/schedule/route.ts:241-318 resolves the trading day for the FOH page today: it fetches business_hours + special_hours in parallel and coalesces `specialHours?.opens ?? businessHours?.opens` (route.ts:303-306), same for closes/kitchen_opens/kitchen_closes, tagging `source: 'fallback' | 'business_hours'` (route.ts:153, 318). The rota is not consulted anywhere in that resolution. special_hours is live and actively maintained — 12 rows in the last ~3 months with real overrides (Easter Monday 14:00, May Day 16:00 kitchen closed, 2026-07-05).
findings.implication: The checklist system should reuse this exact resolver rather than inventing a rota-based one. Since checklists surface on the FOH page (/table-bookings/foh), the resolved opens/closes is already computed and in scope on that screen. Extract the special_hours→business_hours coalesce into a shared helper and call it from both.
findings.blocker: No — but it means the 'rota not business_hours' instruction directly contradicts the existing, working, actively-maintained mechanism. Worth putting to the owner explicitly.

findings.topic: rota_shifts vs rota_published_shifts — which to read
findings.detail: rota_shifts (supabase/migrations/20260228100000_rota_system.sql:50-73) is the live manager draft. rota_published_shifts (supabase/migrations/20260503000002_published_shifts_snapshot.sql:5-21) is a snapshot with the SAME id as its rota_shifts row. Publish is delete-then-insert per week via the ADMIN client (src/app/actions/rota.ts:2994-3005; comment at 2884 notes the snapshot table has no write RLS for regular users), then rota_weeks.status flips (rota.ts:3007-3014). Full column lists confirmed in prod: rota_shifts has 30 cols including template_id, sick_reason, original_employee_id/reassigned_from_id/reassigned_at/reassigned_by/reassignment_reason, created_by/created_at/updated_at. rota_published_shifts has 25 cols and DROPS template_id, sick_reason, all reassignment tracking, and created_by — it ADDS published_at. Both carry: employee_id, shift_date, start_time, end_time, unpaid_break_minutes, department, status, notes, is_overnight, is_open_shift, name, acceptance_* (5), rate_multiplier/rate_override/premium_* (5). Note rota_published_shifts EXCLUDES status='cancelled' rows at publish time (rota.ts:2879).
findings.implication: Read rota_published_shifts. It is what staff have actually been told to work (the portal reads it: src/app/(staff-portal)/portal/shifts/page.tsx:331), so accountability ('you did not do the close') is defensible against it — you cannot hold someone to a draft edit they never saw. rota_shifts would let a manager retro-edit the draft and silently change who was accountable. Cost of reading the snapshot: no template_id (so you cannot join back to rota_shift_templates — but `name` is denormalised onto the row, use that), and no sick_reason. IMPORTANT: status='sick' rows DO survive into the snapshot (only 'cancelled' is filtered), which matters — 'Couldn't Work' absences are rota_shifts rows with status='sick', 15 since May.
findings.blocker: No — recommend rota_published_shifts, with a documented fallback (below) when the week is unpublished.

findings.topic: is_overnight exists but is a display/pay flag, not a trading-day boundary
findings.detail: rota_shifts.is_overnight BOOLEAN NOT NULL DEFAULT FALSE, commented 'TRUE when end_time is on the following calendar day' (20260228100000_rota_system.sql:63, 75). Present on both tables. In prod: 129 of 1516 rota_shifts rows are overnight (8.5%), but ZERO in the sampled 2026-07-06..07-26 window. Usage is confined to paid-hours maths and a '(+1)' suffix in the UI — src/app/(authenticated)/rota/RotaGrid.tsx:270, 325, 354; ShiftDetailModal.tsx:185, 202, 227, 295; portal/shifts/page.tsx:353, 365, 698, 719, 778, 785. It is set manually via a checkbox in ShiftDetailModal (state at :185, written at :227) — it is NOT derived from end_time < start_time.
findings.implication: Two consequences. (1) Any 'latest end' calculation MUST treat is_overnight rows as next-day, or a 00:00 end sorts as the earliest time of the day — my own query had to filter them out to get sane results. (2) Because it is hand-set, it can be wrong; a template like the (inactive) 'Saturday Close' 18:00–00:00 relies on a human ticking the box. Do not trust it as the definition of 'the trading day ended'.
findings.blocker: No.

findings.topic: Shift template naming is NOT reliably Day-Open / Day-Close — two live bar days have no Open/Close split at all
findings.detail: Live prod rota_shift_templates, ACTIVE only. BAR: 'Sunday Open' 12:00–17:00, 'Saturday Open' 12:00–18:00, 'Monday' 16:00–22:00, 'Thursday Open' 16:00–18:00, 'Friday Open' 16:00–19:00, 'Tuesday Open' 16:00–18:00, 'Wednesday' 16:00–22:00, 'Sunday Close' 17:00–22:00, 'Tuesday Close' 18:00–22:00, 'Thursday Close' 18:00–22:00, 'Saturday Close' 18:00–22:00, 'Friday Close' 19:00–22:00. KITCHEN: 'Sunday Lunch Kitchen' 11:30–18:00, 'Saturday Kitchen' 12:00–21:00, 'Sunday Kitchen' 12:00–18:00, 'Wednesday Kitchen Open' 16:00–18:00, 'Friday Kitchen' 16:00–21:00, 'Tuesday Kitchen' 16:00–21:00, 'Thursday Kitchen' 16:00–21:00, 'Wednesday Kitchen' 18:00–21:00. CLEANING: 'Cleaning' 10:30–11:30. HOST: 'Host' 19:00–22:00. RUNNER: 'Sunday Runner' 11:30–18:00, 'Friday Runner' 18:00–21:00, 'Wednesday Runner' 18:00–21:00. CRITICAL: Monday and Wednesday bar are SINGLE all-day templates named just 'Monday' / 'Wednesday' (16:00–22:00) — one person opens AND closes, and the name contains neither 'Open' nor 'Close'. Also there are DUPLICATE names distinguished only by is_active (two 'Saturday Close', two 'Friday Close', two 'Sunday Kitchen', two 'Tuesday Kitchen', two 'Thursday Kitchen', two 'Friday Kitchen'), and inactive Monday/Wednesday/Thursday Open+Close pairs — i.e. they USED to be split and were merged. rota_shifts.name is denormalised from the template (20260503000000_rota_shift_name.sql) and prod name counts since 2026-05-01 confirm the shape: 'Wednesday' 17, 'Monday' 17, alongside 'Saturday Close' 19, 'Friday Open' 18 etc. 13 rows have name=NULL. 15 rows are named "Couldn't Work".
findings.implication: You CANNOT identify the closer by matching name ILIKE '%Close%' — it silently returns nobody on Mondays and Wednesdays (2 of 7 days), and NULL/'Couldn't Work' names break it further. Match on TIME instead: the closer is the employee whose shift end_time is the latest for that date (excluding pre-open cleaning). Do not match on template_id either — it is absent from rota_published_shifts. Also, template names are user-editable free text with no uniqueness constraint, so any name-based logic is a latent bug.
findings.blocker: YES (design-level) — 'the person CLOSING is responsible for all checks' needs a definition that survives Monday/Wednesday. Recommend: closer = the scheduled (non-cancelled, non-sick) shift with the latest end_time on that date, tie-broken by department='bar', with a manual override on the close screen.

findings.topic: Departments: FIVE live, not two — and 'tagged BAR or KITCHEN' will orphan 126 shifts
findings.detail: Live departments table: bar (Bar, sort 0), kitchen (Kitchen, 1), runner (Runner, 2), host (Host, 3), cleaning (Cleaning, 4). Prod rota_shifts department counts: bar 1113, kitchen 277, runner 91, cleaning 18, host 17. Constraint history matters: rota_shifts' department CHECK was DROPPED (20260301120000_rota_shifts_drop_department_check.sql — confirmed by the comment in 20260306000001_rota_department_constraint.sql:3), so rota_shifts.department is now free TEXT with NO check and NO FK to departments. rota_shift_templates' CHECK was widened to ('bar','kitchen','runner') by 20260306000001, then DROPPED entirely by 20260315000001_rota_shift_templates_drop_department_check.sql — which is how the live 'host' and 'cleaning' templates exist. rota_published_shifts.department is also plain TEXT, no constraint (20260503000002:12). department is a per-shift column, not a per-employee one — the same person can work bar one day and kitchen the next.
findings.implication: Tagging tasks BAR/KITCHEN only does not 'tie into how the rota works' — it ignores runner/host/cleaning (126 shifts, 8%). Two specific breaks: (1) the 'Cleaning' 10:30–11:30 shift is department='cleaning' and is the EARLIEST person on site each Sunday — if open tasks are bar/kitchen-only, the person physically opening the building is invisible to the checklist. (2) A kitchen-tagged task on a day with no kitchen shift rostered (e.g. Monday — is_kitchen_closed=true in business_hours for day_of_week=1) has nobody to assign it to. Since there is no FK, checklist code must tolerate unexpected department strings rather than switch exhaustively.
findings.blocker: YES — owner must decide whether checklist tags are exactly the 5 departments (recommend: FK task tags to the departments table, so adding a department later doesn't need a migration) or a separate bar/kitchen-only taxonomy that maps loosely. Recommend the FK.

findings.topic: Publish horizon and the unpublished-week fallback
findings.detail: Today 2026-07-17. Published weeks run through week_start 2026-08-10 (covers to 2026-08-16) = ~4 weeks / 30 days ahead. Beyond that: rota_weeks rows exist for 2026-08-17 and 2026-08-24 with status='draft' and 23 shifts EACH (drafted but not published), and rows for 2026-08-31 / 09-07 / 09-14 / 09-21 / 09-28 with status='draft' and ZERO shifts — i.e. empty placeholder weeks. So there are three distinct states, not two: published-with-shifts, draft-with-shifts, and draft-empty. Historical publish lag is NOT measurable from this data: published_at is OVERWRITTEN on every re-publish (src/app/actions/rota.ts:3009), so weeks show published_at AFTER their own week_start (2026-06-08 published 2026-06-13; 2026-05-11 published 2026-05-21; 2026-07-06 published 2026-07-09) — those are last-touch dates, not first-publish. Also rota_weeks.has_unpublished_changes=true right now for 2026-07-27, meaning the draft has drifted from the snapshot mid-horizon.
findings.implication: A checklist that hard-depends on a published rota is fine ~4 weeks out but has nothing beyond. For a FUTURE date with no published rota you cannot know who is accountable — so do not generate per-person accountability ahead of time. Generate the checklist INSTANCE lazily on the day (or T-1) from the snapshot, and resolve the responsible closer at that point. Fallback chain: rota_published_shifts → (if week unpublished) show the checklist with NO named accountability and let whoever is clocked in claim it → never invent a closer. The has_unpublished_changes=true case means a published snapshot can be stale relative to the manager's intent; on-the-day generation minimises that window.
findings.blocker: YES (narrow) — owner must accept 'no rota published = checklist still appears, but nobody is pre-blamed'. The alternative (fall back to rota_shifts draft) makes accountability rest on a plan staff never saw. Recommend the former.

findings.topic: Timeclock attribution is viable but the clocked-in list is thin and sometimes empty
findings.detail: timeclock_sessions is live and used daily: 1222 sessions, 23 distinct employees, work_date range 2024-12-20 to 2026-07-16, 33 sessions since 2026-07-01. Columns: id, employee_id, work_date, clock_in_at, clock_out_at, linked_shift_id, is_unscheduled, is_auto_close, auto_close_reason, is_reviewed, reviewed_by, reviewed_at, created_at, updated_at, notes, manager_note, rate_multiplier, rate_override, premium_reason, premium_start_at, premium_end_at. 'Currently clocked in' = clock_out_at IS NULL. Daily clocked-vs-rostered headcount for 2026-07-01..07-16: typically 1–3 clocked against 1–6 rostered; clocked < rostered on 10 of 16 days (e.g. 07-11: 2 clocked vs 4 rostered; 07-05: 4 vs 6); on 07-06, 2 clocked vs 1 rostered (unscheduled cover). At the moment of query, open_now = 0.
findings.implication: Three things for the no-PIN attribution UI. (1) The clocked-in list is often 1–2 names — good, a fast tick. (2) It is regularly SHORTER than the rostered list, so people who worked will be missing from it; the picker must let you add anyone rostered that day, not just the clocked-in. (3) It can legitimately be EMPTY (nobody clocked in yet, or an auto-close already fired — is_auto_close/auto_close_reason exist, so sessions get force-closed). An empty picker must not block ticking the task. Also linked_shift_id gives you a direct join from a clock-in to its rota shift, which is the cleanest bridge between 'who is here' and 'who was rostered to close'.
findings.blocker: No — but recommend the attribution picker be: clocked-in first (pre-ticked/prominent), then rostered-but-not-clocked-in, then a search-all fallback.

findings.topic: Employee audit log target for spot checks
findings.detail: There is no dedicated employee audit table. The audit-log tables in prod are: audit_logs (the general one, written via logAuditEvent() per the workspace Supabase rules), plus domain-specific booking_audit, invoice_audit, private_booking_audit. There is no employee_audit / employee_audit_log table.
findings.implication: 'Spot check result must be tracked on the EMPLOYEE AUDIT LOG' means one of: write to the generic audit_logs with resource_type='employee' and the employee id as resource_id (cheapest, consistent with existing convention), or add a purpose-built spot_check table that the employee record renders from. Note audit_logs is an operational/system log keyed by the acting USER — Billy — not by the employee being checked, so a naive logAuditEvent() call records Billy's action, not a mark against the checked employee. To make it show on the checked person's record you must set resource_id to their employee_id and accept that the log then mixes 'who did it' and 'who it was about'.
findings.blocker: YES (small) — owner/architect must pick the target. Recommend a dedicated spot_checks table (it has real domain fields: checker, checked employee, task instance, pass/fail, note, date) PLUS a logAuditEvent() call for the trail, and surface spot_checks on the employee record. Overloading audit_logs alone will make the 'per-person scoring' query awkward and will not survive audit-log retention/pruning.

findings.topic: business_hours shape — every day closes 22:00, and close-shift ends coincide exactly
findings.detail: Live business_hours (all 7 days, is_closed=false): Sun opens 12:00 closes 22:00 (kitchen 13:00–18:00, sunday_lunch schedule_config cap 50); Mon 16:00–22:00 (is_kitchen_closed=TRUE, kitchen_opens/closes NULL); Tue/Wed/Thu 16:00–22:00 (kitchen 16:00–21:00); Fri 16:00–22:00 (kitchen 16:00–21:00, but schedule_config has a lunch slot 12:00–14:30 — inconsistent with opens=16:00); Sat 12:00–22:00 (kitchen 12:00–19:00, schedule_config dinner slot ends 21:00). Columns: id, day_of_week (0=Sun), opens, closes, kitchen_opens, kitchen_closes, is_closed, is_kitchen_closed, schedule_config (jsonb), created_at, updated_at. All rows last updated 2026-03-22. Validation lives in src/services/business-hours.ts (zod businessHoursSchema, requires opens/closes when not is_closed).
findings.implication: Because closes=22:00 on every day AND the active bar Close templates all end at 22:00, max(shift end) == closes on most days — which makes the rota LOOK like a valid source of close time. It isn't; it coincides. The moment a close shift is short-rostered (Sat 2026-07-11: latest end 21:00) or a day is unrostered, the derivation silently returns the wrong answer with no error. Separately: Mon is_kitchen_closed=TRUE means kitchen-tagged tasks must be suppressed on Mondays, and that fact lives in business_hours, not the rota. Fri's 12:00 lunch schedule_config against a 16:00 open is an existing data inconsistency worth flagging to the owner but out of scope here.
findings.blocker: No — but it is the reason the 'rota as source of truth' idea looks plausible in a spot-check and fails in production.

findings.topic: "The employee audit log" is really three separate things today
findings.detail: The employee record page renders tabs at src/app/(authenticated)/employees/[employee_id]/page.tsx:160-279 plus, below the tabs, a Notes section (page.tsx:393-404, EmployeeNotesList) and an EmployeeAuditTrail component (imported page.tsx:22). EmployeeAuditTrail (src/components/features/employees/EmployeeAuditTrail.tsx:25-44) MERGES two sources into one timeline: rows from `audit_logs` and rows from `employee_notes`. So the thing the owner calls "the employee audit log" is a merged audit+notes timeline on the employee record.
findings.implication: A spot-check result can be made to appear on the employee record by writing to EITHER audit_logs (with the right resource_type/resource_id) or employee_notes — both already surface in the same timeline. No new UI is strictly required to make it visible.

findings.topic: audit_logs IS queryable per-employee, but only by string convention
findings.detail: Table defined at supabase/migrations/20251123120000_squashed.sql:1746-1762: id, created_at, user_id, user_email, operation_type, resource_type, resource_id (TEXT), operation_status ('success'|'failure' CHECK), ip_address, user_agent, old_values, new_values, error_message, additional_info (jsonb). There is NO FK to employees. The per-employee fetch is src/services/employees.ts:1019 — `.from('audit_logs').select('*').eq('resource_type','employee').eq('resource_id', employeeId).order('created_at', desc).limit(50)`. user_id is the ACTOR's auth user, not the subject employee.
findings.implication: To land on the employee record, a spot-check audit row MUST use resource_type:'employee' and resource_id: <employee_id UUID as text>. The subject is identified only by resource_id; the actor (Billy) lands in user_id/user_email. Note the hard `.limit(50)` — 2 spot checks/day would flood the timeline and push older audit entries out of view within weeks.
findings.blocker: Decision needed: if spot checks go into audit_logs, the 50-row cap in src/services/employees.ts:1019 has to be raised or filtered, or the timeline becomes useless.

findings.topic: logAuditEvent signature and behaviour
findings.detail: Thin server-action wrapper at src/app/actions/audit.ts:5 delegating to AuditService.logAuditEvent (src/services/audit.ts:18-80). Params type AuditLogParams (src/services/audit.ts:5-16): { user_id?, user_email?, operation_type: string, resource_type: string, resource_id?, operation_status: 'success'|'failure', error_message?, old_values?, new_values?, additional_info? }. operation_type/resource_type are free text (no enum/CHECK) — squashed.sql:1772-1776 documents them only as comments. It uses createAdminClient() (bypasses RLS), auto-resolves user_email from user_id, and captures IP/user-agent from headers(). It NEVER throws — all errors are console.error'd and swallowed.
findings.implication: logAuditEvent is fire-and-forget: a failed write is silent. Anything that must be provably recorded (a spot-check result) cannot rely on logAuditEvent alone — it needs its own row in a real table, with the audit entry as a secondary trail.

findings.topic: employee_notes is the existing manager-observation-against-a-person mechanism
findings.detail: Table at supabase/migrations/20251123120000_squashed.sql:2213-2219: note_id, employee_id (FK → employees.employee_id ON DELETE CASCADE, squashed.sql:4495-4496), note_text, created_at, created_by_user_id. Write path: addEmployeeNote server action at src/app/actions/employeeActions.ts:530-565 → checkUserPermission('employees','edit') → EmployeeService.addEmployeeNote (src/services/employees.ts:556-559, uses adminClient) → then logAuditEvent({ operation_type:'add_note', resource_type:'employee', resource_id: employee_id, additional_info:{ note_preview } }). There's also a mobile QuickAddNoteSheet (page.tsx:10).
findings.implication: This is the closest precedent to "manager records an observation about a person" and is exactly the double-write pattern to copy: real row + logAuditEvent mirror with resource_type:'employee'.

findings.topic: employee_notes RLS is effectively open — do not use it as the system of record for a scored observation
findings.detail: squashed.sql:4903 "Users can create notes" FOR INSERT TO authenticated WITH CHECK (true); :5037 "Users can view notes" FOR SELECT USING (true); :4915/:4975 allow the author to UPDATE and DELETE their own notes. Grants at :5674-5676 include anon.
findings.implication: Any authenticated user can read every employee note, and an author can silently edit or delete their own. Spot-check results must not live only here — Billy's record would be mutable and world-readable to all staff logins.

findings.topic: employee_reliability_events is the best-fitting existing home pattern
findings.detail: supabase/migrations/20260706000000_employee_reliability_events.sql:3-41 — per-employee append-only event log: employee_id FK, event_type (CHECK enum of 9 rota/leave values), event_at, source, source_table, source_id, idempotency_key UNIQUE, shift_id, shift_date, department, score_eligible BOOLEAN, note TEXT, metadata JSONB. RLS is read-only to `employees:view` holders (migration :62-68) with no INSERT policy for authenticated — writes go via service role in src/services/employee-reliability.ts:147-205 (recordReliabilityEvent, upsert onConflict idempotency_key). It already has a dedicated employee tab: 'Reliability' at page.tsx:278-279 via EmployeeReliabilityTab, fed by getEmployeeReliabilityData. The service ALSO double-writes an audit trail (recordEmployeeReliabilityAudit, src/services/employee-reliability.ts:207+).
findings.implication: This is a proven per-person, per-event, scored, append-only, service-role-write table with an employee-record tab already wired in. It is the shape a spot-check result should take. But its event_type CHECK constraint is closed and its columns are rota/leave-specific (shift_id, leave_request_id, notice_days) — jamming spot checks in would need a CHECK-constraint migration and would pollute the reliability score.
findings.blocker: Decision: extend employee_reliability_events' event_type enum, or create a sibling table. Recommend a sibling (see below).

findings.topic: Recommended home for a spot-check result
findings.detail: Create `checklist_spot_checks` modelled on employee_reliability_events + recruitment_candidate_notes: id, task_completion_id (FK to the original tick), employee_id (FK employees ON DELETE CASCADE — one row per credited person if multiple), result ('pass'|'fail' CHECK), checked_by_user_id (auth.users), checked_at timestamptz, note text, department ('bar'|'kitchen'), created_at. Append-only: SELECT policy for user_has_permission(auth.uid(),'employees','view'), no UPDATE/DELETE policy, service-role FOR ALL — exactly the recruitment_candidate_notes pattern (supabase/migrations/20260717000000_recruitment_candidate_notes.sql:18-30). On insert, ALSO call logAuditEvent({ user_id: Billy, operation_type:'spot_check_fail', resource_type:'employee', resource_id: employeeId, operation_status:'success', additional_info:{ taskName, result, note } }) so it lands in the existing merged EmployeeAuditTrail timeline with zero new UI.
findings.implication: Satisfies "tracked on the employee audit log" via the audit_logs mirror, while keeping an immutable, permission-gated source of record that the original tick is not mutated by (owner's rule: a failed spot check does not cancel the original tick). Cross-check: the double-write must be inside a try/catch-independent path since logAuditEvent swallows errors.

findings.topic: Precedent for immutable manager-authored notes: recruitment_candidate_notes
findings.detail: supabase/migrations/20260717000000_recruitment_candidate_notes.sql:4-13 — id, candidate_id FK, application_id FK, content, kind (default 'note'), created_by (auth.users FK), created_by_email, created_at. Header comment explicitly states: no UPDATE/DELETE policy is defined, so notes are immutable at the DB layer. RLS: read on recruitment:view, insert on recruitment:edit, service_role FOR ALL. Actions in src/app/actions/recruitment.ts, service in src/services/recruitment.ts. NOTE this migration is dated 20260717 — today — so it may be brand new/uncommitted; verify it is actually applied to prod before citing it as shipped precedent.
findings.implication: This is the exact template for the spot-check table: append-only, attributed to both auth user id AND a denormalised email (which survives the user being deleted), permission-gated by module. The `kind` column pattern also shows how to distinguish note types without a new table.

findings.topic: There is no employee↔auth.users FK anywhere in the audit path
findings.detail: audit_logs.user_id points at auth.users (the actor). employee_notes.created_by_user_id is a bare uuid with no FK (squashed.sql:2213-2219 declares no constraint on it). employee_reliability_events has employee_id FK to employees but no actor column at all. The per-employee audit fetch (src/services/employees.ts:1019) matches on resource_id text only.
findings.implication: For the checklists feature's "show everyone currently clocked in so they can tick their name" attribution, the credited party is an EMPLOYEE (employees.employee_id), while the audit actor is an AUTH USER. These are different keys and must not be conflated. The spot-check table needs both: employee_id (who is credited/failed) and checked_by_user_id (Billy).

findings.topic: Employee version history exists as a separate view
findings.detail: squallshed.sql:2224+ defines VIEW `employee_version_history` built from audit_logs (al.id, al.created_at, al.user_id, al.user_email...). Actions in src/app/actions/employee-history.ts:12-55 (getEmployeeChangesSummary → RPC get_employee_changes_summary, permission-gated on employees:view) and a restoreEmployeeVersion path. Component EmployeeRecentChanges (page.tsx:23).
findings.implication: This view is derived from audit_logs. Writing spot-check rows into audit_logs with resource_type:'employee' will also flow into version-history surfaces — check get_employee_changes_summary and the view's WHERE clause before shipping, or spot checks may show up as bogus 'field changes' in EmployeeRecentChanges.
findings.blocker: Must verify the employee_version_history view / get_employee_changes_summary filter (by operation_type?) before adding a new operation_type to audit_logs against resource_type 'employee'.

findings.topic: timeclock_sessions schema (migration + live schema match)
findings.detail: Defined at /Users/peterpitcher/Cursor/OJ-AnchorManagementTools/supabase/migrations/20260228100000_rota_system.sql:135-158. Live prod columns (project tfcasgxopxegwrabvwat) confirm: id, employee_id (FK employees.employee_id, ON DELETE CASCADE), work_date (date, Europe/London date of clock_in_at), clock_in_at (timestamptz NOT NULL), clock_out_at (timestamptz NULL = OPEN SESSION), linked_shift_id (FK rota_shifts, SET NULL), is_unscheduled, is_auto_close, auto_close_reason ('scheduled_end'|'fallback_0500'), is_reviewed, reviewed_by (auth.users), reviewed_at, created_at, updated_at, plus later additions: notes, manager_note, rate_multiplier, rate_override, premium_reason, premium_start_at, premium_end_at. Partial index idx_timeclock_sessions_open ON (employee_id) WHERE clock_out_at IS NULL exists, so the open-session lookup is cheap. NOTE: there is NO unique constraint enforcing one open session per employee — clockIn relies on a SELECT-then-INSERT check (timeclock.ts:194-206) and a 23505 catch that no constraint actually raises.
findings.implication: An OPEN session = clock_out_at IS NULL. 'Currently clocked in' = SELECT ... FROM timeclock_sessions WHERE clock_out_at IS NULL, optionally joined to employees for names. Checklists can safely reference timeclock_sessions.id, but must not rely on a DB-level uniqueness of open sessions.

findings.topic: Existing helper for currently-clocked-in staff already exists — reuse it
findings.detail: getOpenSessions() at /Users/peterpitcher/Cursor/OJ-AnchorManagementTools/src/app/actions/timeclock.ts:314-339 returns every open session joined to employee names ((TimeclockSession & { employee_name: string })[]), ordered by clock_in_at. It uses the SERVICE-ROLE admin client (timeclock.ts:16-18) and performs NO auth or permission check at all. Consumers: /Users/peterpitcher/Cursor/OJ-AnchorManagementTools/src/app/(authenticated)/table-bookings/foh/page.tsx:82 (server-side, feeds FohClockWidget) and /Users/peterpitcher/Cursor/OJ-AnchorManagementTools/src/ds/shell/FohClockBand.tsx:21-22 (client, filters to its own employeeId).
findings.implication: The 'who is clocked in right now' picker for task attribution should call getOpenSessions() — no new query needed. But it is an unauthenticated RLS-bypassing action; if checklists widen its use, add a permission check or a narrower read-only wrapper rather than exposing more staff data.

findings.topic: KILLER FINDING: the person who closes never clocks in — attribution by clocked-in will be empty for closing/kitchen tasks
findings.detail: Live prod, last 30 days of scheduled rota_shifts vs timeclock_sessions on the same employee+date: 179 of 246 scheduled shifts (73%) have NO timeclock session at all. Per person: Billy Summers 46 shifts / 46 with no session (0%% clocked in, all 'kitchen'), Peter Pitcher 9/9 no session, Laura Bradshaw 19/27 no session, Lance Marlow 17/27, Amanda Jones 36/58, Ryan Bond 14/20, Marty Pitcher-Summers 9/13. Also: right now there are 0 open sessions in prod (SELECT count(*) FROM timeclock_sessions WHERE clock_out_at IS NULL = 0), and typical last clock-out is 22:00–22:40 London.
findings.implication: For the OPEN/CLOSE checklists and for anything tagged KITCHEN, 'show everyone currently clocked in' will frequently show an empty or wrong list — Billy (kitchen, and the spot-check owner) has literally never produced a timeclock session. Closing tasks done after ~22:30 will often have zero open sessions. The attribution picker MUST fall back to a wider list (e.g. everyone rota'd for that shift_date, plus a manual 'someone else' search over active employees), with clocked-in people merely pre-ticked at the top.
findings.blocker: Owner decision needed: the 'clocked in right now' list is empty most nights. Recommend: source the picker from rota_shifts for the current work_date (dept-filtered to bar/kitchen), pre-tick anyone with an open timeclock session, and allow free selection of any Active employee. Confirm before building.

findings.topic: How FOH knows which employee is at the iPad — it doesn't, for manager@the-anchor.pub
findings.detail: Two separate mechanisms. (1) FohClockBand: /Users/peterpitcher/Cursor/OJ-AnchorManagementTools/src/app/(authenticated)/AuthenticatedLayout.tsx:102-119 resolves fohEmployeeId by matching employees.email_address to the signed-in auth user's email, and ONLY when isFohOnlyUser(permissions) is true (src/lib/foh/user-mode.ts:7-21 — user must have table_bookings:view and NOTHING outside table_bookings). AppShell only renders the band when fohMode && fohEmployeeId (src/ds/shell/AppShell.tsx:86-87). (2) FohClockWidget on the FOH page (src/app/(authenticated)/table-bookings/foh/FohClockWidget.tsx:34-68) is identity-free: it lists ALL Active employees (foh/page.tsx:81) and lets anyone at the iPad clock anyone in or out.
findings.implication: The FOH iPad has NO notion of 'the current employee'. Anything on the FOH surface must treat the device as shared and anonymous — exactly why the owner's 'tick your name from the clocked-in list' model is the right shape. The FohClockBand path is irrelevant to the iPad; it only lights up for a truly FOH-only staff login.

findings.topic: manager@the-anchor.pub is NOT an employee — no employees row, no auth_user_id link
findings.detail: Live prod: auth.users has manager@the-anchor.pub = 1c8a8e8e-ed99-4de6-9914-e9fc31d9ca50 (created 2026-02-08). SELECT count(*) FROM employees WHERE auth_user_id='1c8a8e8e-...' returns 0, and no employees row has email_address = manager@the-anchor.pub (only billy@orangejelly.co.uk and peter@orangejelly.co.uk match a name search). 11 of 11 Active employees do have auth_user_id set. The code already special-cases this address: MANAGER_IPAD_EMAIL = 'manager@the-anchor.pub' at src/app/actions/timeclock.ts:21, and canUseManagerFohKioskTimeclock() (timeclock.ts:55-66) lets it clock people in/out without a PIN provided it has table_bookings:view.
findings.implication: Never resolve the ticker/actor from the signed-in user on FOH — the iPad account maps to no employee. Checklist completions must store employee_id(s) chosen from the picker, NOT auth user id. Store the acting auth user separately (as a 'recorded via' field) if you want a device/session trail. fohEmployeeId will always be undefined on the manager iPad, so the FohClockBand never renders there.

findings.topic: Auto-close cron cannot close a session mid-closing-list, but it does silently rewrite clock-out times
findings.detail: /Users/peterpitcher/Cursor/OJ-AnchorManagementTools/src/app/api/cron/rota-auto-close/route.ts, scheduled '0 5 * * *' (vercel.json:209-211), and it hard-refuses to run outside 04:00–06:00 London (route.ts:25-32). It finds all sessions with clock_out_at IS NULL and closes them at the LINKED SHIFT'S END TIME (reason 'scheduled_end', route.ts:66-77) or, with no linked shift, at 23:59 London on work_date (reason 'fallback_0500', route.ts:79-85). Prod: only 1 auto-closed session in 1222 total ever.
findings.implication: No risk of closing a session out from under someone doing the closing list at 23:30 — the cron only runs at 05:00. BUT the back-dated clock-out means a session auto-closed at 'scheduled_end' 21:00 will later look as though the person was NOT clocked in at 23:00 when they ticked the closing list. If checklists derive attribution retrospectively (e.g. reporting 'who was on'), snapshot the employee_ids at tick time — do not recompute from timeclock_sessions later.

findings.topic: Concurrency: 1–4 people clocked in at once, typically 2
findings.detail: Live prod, last 60 days, computed by sweeping clock-in/clock-out events per work_date: peak concurrency 1 on 16 days, 2 on 24 days, 3 on 16 days, 4 on 4 days. Max ever observed in the window = 4. Session counts per day range 1–6. Yes, multiple people are clocked in simultaneously and the clockIn guard is per-employee only (timeclock.ts:194-206).
findings.implication: The 'tick your name' picker will show a very short list (0–4 names). A simple checkbox list of open sessions is fine — no search/virtualisation needed. Multi-credit is the norm, so model completions as a join table (checklist_completion_employees) rather than a single employee_id column.

findings.topic: Many sessions are manager-entered after the fact, not live kiosk taps
findings.detail: Live prod, last 60 days: 135 of 142 clock-ins land on an exact whole minute (:00.000 seconds), which a live nowUtc clock-in (timeclock.ts:210-219) essentially never produces. These come from createTimeclockSession() (src/app/actions/timeclock.ts:542) — the manager's manual entry screen. created_at is usually within ~0.1h of clock_in_at, but several are 8–25h later (e.g. 2026-07-15 one entry +8.6h; 2026-07-05 four entries averaging +16.1h).
findings.implication: Reinforces the finding above: the timeclock is largely a payroll record keyed in by a manager, not a live presence signal. Do not treat 'has an open session' as reliable evidence someone is on the premises — it is a nice-to-have pre-tick, not the source of truth.

findings.topic: Rota is queryable as the presence fallback; departments already map to BAR/KITCHEN
findings.detail: rota_shifts live columns include employee_id, shift_date (date), start_time/end_time (time without time zone), is_overnight, department, status, name, sick_reason, acceptance_status. DISTINCT department in prod = bar, cleaning, host, kitchen, runner. linkSessionToShift() (src/app/actions/timeclock.ts:346-359) already matches sessions to shifts by employee + shift_date + status='scheduled' within a 2h window.
findings.implication: The owner's BAR/KITCHEN task tags line up directly with rota_shifts.department (bar vs kitchen), with cleaning/host/runner needing an explicit mapping decision. The rota also gives the open/close responsibility ('who did not do it') and, per the owner, is the source of truth for opening hours — use rota_shifts.start_time/end_time per shift_date rather than business_hours.
findings.blocker: Minor decision: how to tag cleaning/host/runner shifts against BAR/KITCHEN task tags. Recommend treating cleaning/host/runner as BAR — they are all front-of-house in practice.

findings.topic: Spot checks need the employee audit trail — employee_notes is the fit, audit_logs is the system trail
findings.detail: Prod public schema has audit_logs (the generic logAuditEvent target, src/app/actions/audit.ts) and employee_notes (note_id, employee_id, note_text, created_at, created_by_user_id). There is no dedicated employee audit-log table; employee_version_history and employee_reliability_events also exist. logAuditEvent is already called for clock_in/clock_out with resource_type 'timeclock_session' (timeclock.ts:228-235, 288-295).
findings.implication: Billy's spot-check result should write (a) a checklist_spot_checks row holding the verdict, (b) an employee_notes row per credited employee so it shows on their profile ('EMPLOYEE AUDIT LOG'), and (c) a logAuditEvent call. Note employee_notes.created_by_user_id is an auth user id — Billy's login works, but the manager iPad account has no employee row, so spot checks should be recorded from Billy's own login, not the iPad.

findings.topic: RLS: anon can read open sessions; authenticated can do anything
findings.detail: supabase/migrations/20260228100000_rota_system.sql:401-402 grants authenticated FOR ALL USING(true) on timeclock_sessions. Lines 437-445 grant anon INSERT (clock in) and anon SELECT WHERE clock_out_at IS NULL. The anon UPDATE policy was added (20260228000003_timeclock_anon_update_policy.sql) then dropped (20260708000014_drop_timeclock_anon_update.sql).
findings.implication: New checklist tables should follow the same shape: RLS on, authenticated FOR ALL, and no anon policies at all — the FOH surface runs inside (authenticated) as manager@the-anchor.pub, so it does not need anon access. Do not copy the anon INSERT policy.

findings.topic: Modal — canonical component
findings.detail: `Modal` lives at src/ds/primitives/Modal.tsx:38, exported from `@/ds`. Headless UI Dialog + Transition, props `{ open, onClose, title?, children, footer?, width|size: 'sm'|'md'|'lg'|'xl' }`. Focus trap + Escape come free from Headless UI. It is bottom-sheet on mobile, centred on sm+ (Modal.tsx:66,78). Siblings: `ConfirmDialog` (src/ds/primitives/ConfirmDialog.tsx:1, 116 lines) and `Drawer` (src/ds/primitives/Drawer.tsx). `ModalActions` is the footer wrapper. Canonical in-FOH usage: src/app/(authenticated)/table-bookings/foh/components/FohMiniModals.tsx:26-66 (`FohPartySizeModal` — Modal + size="sm" + footer=<ModalActions> + a numeric input, React.memo'd).
findings.implication: Use `Modal` from `@/ds` for the mid-shift task modal. Copy FohMiniModals.tsx:26-66 verbatim as the shape. Use `ConfirmDialog` for destructive confirms, not a hand-rolled modal.

findings.topic: Modal that fires on a timer / page state — no existing pattern, but all the parts exist
findings.detail: There is NO existing 'auto-open modal at time X' pattern in the codebase. But the ingredients are all in FohScheduleClient.tsx: (a) a minute-aligned clock tick at src/app/(authenticated)/table-bookings/foh/FohScheduleClient.tsx:150 — `setTimeout` to the next minute boundary then `setInterval(tick, 60_000)` writing `clockNow` state; (b) an idle/auto-return interval at FohScheduleClient.tsx:180-193 which gates on `hasActiveFohWork` (FohScheduleClient.tsx:174-178), `document.visibilityState !== 'visible'`, and whether the active element is an input/textarea/select/contenteditable before it acts.
findings.implication: The mid-shift task modal should subscribe to the existing `clockNow` minute tick rather than adding a new timer, and MUST copy the FohScheduleClient.tsx:180-193 guard set — never pop a modal while a booking action is in flight, another modal is open, or the user is typing. `hasActiveFohWork` is the existing boolean to extend.

findings.topic: FOH iPad staying on one page all night — polling + realtime already exist
findings.detail: src/app/(authenticated)/table-bookings/foh/hooks/useFohRealtime.ts is a dedicated hook that (a) opens a Supabase realtime channel `foh-schedule-live-${date}` subscribed to postgres_changes on 8 tables (useFohRealtime.ts:66-108), (b) debounces refresh by 500ms (line 58), (c) suppresses refresh while dragging (line 53), and (d) runs a 60-second polling fallback: `pollIntervalId = window.setInterval(queueRefresh, 60_000)` at useFohRealtime.ts:110. Consumed at FohScheduleClient.tsx:26. Separately the page auto-returns to today after idle (FOH_AUTO_RETURN_IDLE_MS / FOH_AUTO_RETURN_POLL_MS, imported at FohScheduleClient.tsx:18-19).
findings.implication: The iPad-sits-all-night problem is already solved. Add `checklist_*` tables to the existing channel in useFohRealtime.ts:66-108 (or add a parallel `useChecklistDue` hook following the same shape) so due tasks appear without a reload. Do NOT invent a new polling mechanism.

findings.topic: Dedicated screen — PageLayout is the standard, but two competing conventions exist
findings.detail: `PageLayout` (src/ds/composites/PageLayout.tsx:117-139) is the full prop surface: title, subtitle, breadcrumbs, backButton, headerActions, showHeaderActionsOnMobile, hideMobileMenuButton, compactHeader, navItems, navActions, toolbar, padded, containerSize, loading, loadingLabel, error, onRetry, className/headerClassName/contentClassName. It has built-in loading and error states (lines 132-135). Best example of a single full page: src/app/(authenticated)/table-bookings/foh/page.tsx:110-133. But the newest multi-page section (cashing-up) uses a DIFFERENT pattern: a section layout with `PageHeader` + `SectionNav` (src/app/(authenticated)/cashing-up/layout.tsx:17-23) and bare server pages beneath it (src/app/(authenticated)/cashing-up/daily/page.tsx), with permission gating in the layout: `const canView = await checkUserPermission('cashing_up','view'); if (!canView) redirect('/unauthorized')` (cashing-up/layout.tsx:14-15).
findings.implication: Checklists has multiple screens (open/close screen + superadmin setup + insights), so follow the cashing-up shape: `/checklists/layout.tsx` with PageHeader + SectionNav + a single `checklists` permission check, and thin server `page.tsx` files delegating to `_components/*Client.tsx`. Use PageLayout only if it ends up a single screen.
findings.blocker: Pick one: cashing-up section-layout pattern vs PageLayout-per-page. Recommend cashing-up pattern — it matches the multi-screen shape and centralises the permission redirect.

findings.topic: FOH page — exactly where the new button goes
findings.detail: Two candidate slots. (1) `PageLayout headerActions` at src/app/(authenticated)/table-bookings/foh/page.tsx:88-108 — currently the manager-kiosk clock widget + logo, or a `<LinkButton href="/settings/table-bookings" variant="secondary" size="sm">Table Setup</LinkButton>` gated on `canManageSettings` (page.tsx:102-108). Note `showHeaderActionsOnMobile` is only true for the kiosk (page.tsx:120), so a non-kiosk headerActions button gets demoted into the nav row (PageLayout.tsx:174). (2) FohHeader's action row at src/app/(authenticated)/table-bookings/foh/components/FohHeader.tsx:225-297 — this holds Food Order (lines 227-247), the Inside/Outside view toggle (250-267), Walk-in (271-283) and Add booking (284-294). All are raw `<button>` with hand-rolled Tailwind, all `cn()`-branched on `isManagerKioskStyle`. Exact Walk-in code, FohHeader.tsx:271-283: `className={cn('rounded-md px-4 py-2 text-sm text-white', isManagerKioskStyle ? 'bg-sidebar px-2.5 py-1 text-[11px] font-semibold hover:bg-green-700' : 'bg-sidebar font-medium hover:bg-sidebar/90')}`. Add booking (284-294) is the secondary variant: `'rounded-md border border-gray-300 bg-white px-4 py-2 text-sm font-medium text-gray-700 hover:bg-gray-50'`.
findings.implication: Put the Checklists button in the FohHeader action row (FohHeader.tsx:225-297), next to Walk-in/Add booking — it is a floor action, and it is the only slot guaranteed visible on the manager kiosk iPad. Match the Add-booking secondary classes verbatim and add the `isManagerKioskStyle` compact branch. It needs a new prop on FohHeaderProps (FohHeader.tsx:9-33) and it should call `lastInteractionAtMsRef.current = Date.now()` on click like every other control there (e.g. FohHeader.tsx:61, 183). A due-task count badge belongs here too, mirroring `Outside ({outsideCount})` at line 265.
findings.blocker: FohHeader uses raw buttons, not `@/ds` Button. Match the local raw-button style (consistency within the screen) rather than introducing `@/ds` Button mid-row.

findings.topic: isFohOnlyUser — exact code
findings.detail: src/lib/foh/user-mode.ts, whole file: `export function isFohOnlyUser(permissions: UserPermission[]): boolean` returns false when permissions is empty/not an array; requires a `{module_name:'table_bookings', action:'view'}` permission; then returns `permissions.every(p => p.module_name === 'table_bookings')`. So FOH-only == every permission the user holds is on the `table_bookings` module. Same file also has `isPortalOnlyUser` (permissions.length === 0). There is no test file — src/lib/foh/ contains only api-auth.ts, bookings.ts, user-mode.ts.
findings.implication: CRITICAL: if you grant an FOH-only user any `checklists` permission, `isFohOnlyUser` returns FALSE — they lose chromeless mode entirely and get the full sidebar. This function must change no matter what.
findings.blocker: Decide the permission model. Recommend: add `checklists` to an allowed-module set inside user-mode.ts, e.g. `const FOH_MODULES = new Set(['table_bookings','checklists'])` and change the final line to `permissions.every(p => FOH_MODULES.has(p.module_name))`, keeping the `table_bookings:view` requirement as the anchor. Alternative (no user-mode change): do not create a `checklists` module and gate checklists on `table_bookings:view` — cheaper, but then superadmin setup screens have no distinct permission.

findings.topic: The AuthenticatedLayout redirect — exact code and exactly what must change
findings.detail: src/app/(authenticated)/AuthenticatedLayout.tsx:35 `const isFohPath = pathname.startsWith('/table-bookings/foh')`. Line 31-34 computes `fohOnlyMode` via `isFohOnlyUser(permissions)`. The redirect effect, lines 92-100: `if (loading || !user || permissionsLoading || !fohOnlyMode) return; if (!isFohPath) router.replace('/table-bookings/foh')`. A render-time guard at lines 130-136 additionally returns a 'Redirecting to Front of House...' screen for any non-FOH path. Chromeless mode is driven by the same flag at lines 170-173: `<AppShell showSidebar={!fohOnlyMode} fohMode={fohOnlyMode} fohEmployeeId={fohEmployeeId} ...>`.
findings.implication: Two precise changes, and only two: (1) replace the single-prefix `isFohPath` at AuthenticatedLayout.tsx:35 with an allowlist test, e.g. `const FOH_ALLOWED_PREFIXES = ['/table-bookings/foh', '/checklists']` and `const isFohPath = FOH_ALLOWED_PREFIXES.some(p => pathname.startsWith(p))`; and (2) leave lines 170-173 untouched — `showSidebar`/`fohMode` key off `fohOnlyMode`, NOT off `isFohPath`, so chromeless mode is preserved on /checklists automatically. The render guard at 130-136 keys off the same `isFohPath` so it fixes itself. Also note src/app/(authenticated)/layout.tsx:75 redirects zero-permission users to /portal/shifts before any of this — unaffected.

findings.topic: Existing allowlist concept
findings.detail: There is no route allowlist for FOH-only users — AuthenticatedLayout.tsx:35 is a single hardcoded `startsWith('/table-bookings/foh')`. The nearest existing allowlist idiom is src/middleware.ts:10 `const PUBLIC_PATH_PREFIXES = [...]` matched at line 45 via `pathname === prefix || pathname.startsWith(`${prefix}/`)`. Note `isFohOnlyUser` is imported by 6 call sites: AuthenticatedLayout.tsx and table-bookings/{page,foh/page,boh/page,[id]/page,reports/page}.tsx.
findings.implication: Introduce the FOH allowlist as a named const in src/lib/foh/user-mode.ts (e.g. `FOH_ALLOWED_PREFIXES` + an `isFohAllowedPath(pathname)` helper) following the middleware.ts:10/45 idiom, so future additions are one line. Do NOT touch middleware — /checklists is authenticated, not public. The other 5 call sites only use `isFohOnlyUser` to hide nav/back-buttons, so widening the module set there is behaviourally safe but worth a diff check.

findings.topic: Forms — numeric input + notes textarea house standard
findings.detail: Canonical example: src/app/(authenticated)/mgd/_components/CollectionForm.tsx — imports `{ Button, Input, Field, Textarea, toast } from '@/ds'` (line 3), plain `useState` per field + `saving` + `error` (lines 31-42), no react-hook-form/zod on the client. Numeric field (CollectionForm.tsx:94-106): ` setNetTake(e.target.value)} icon={£} placeholder="0.00" required disabled={disabled} />`. Notes field (131-138): `
findings.implication: For the temperature-value tasks: `°C} .../>` plus a `

findings.topic: Attribution — 'show everyone currently clocked in' already has a server action and a UI precedent
findings.detail: `getOpenSessions()` at src/app/actions/timeclock.ts:314-339 returns `{success:true, data:(TimeclockSession & {employee_name:string})[]}` — selects `timeclock_sessions` where `clock_out_at is null`, embeds `employees!timeclock_sessions_employee_id_fkey(first_name,last_name)`, and flattens to `employee_name`. It is already called server-side from the FOH page for the kiosk: src/app/(authenticated)/table-bookings/foh/page.tsx:82 (`getOpenSessions()` inside a Promise.all, result passed as `initialSessions` to FohClockWidget at page.tsx:91). `FohClockWidget` (src/app/(authenticated)/table-bookings/foh/FohClockWidget.tsx:34-68) is the working precedent for a Modal-based employee picker over open sessions, incl. `clockedInIds = new Set(sessions.map(s => s.employee_id))` (line 42). Also relevant: `linkSessionToShift` (timeclock.ts:346+) already joins a timeclock session to its `rota_shifts` row by employee + shift_date + clock-in within 2h.
findings.implication: Do not build a new clocked-in query. Call `getOpenSessions()` and render the multi-select tick-list from it, mirroring FohClockWidget.tsx:34-68. Caveat: getOpenSessions uses the cookie client (RLS applies), and page.tsx:82 only calls it for the manager-kiosk email — the checklist screen will need its own call. `linkSessionToShift` is the existing bridge from a clock-in to a rota shift, which is what the open/close 'who did not do it' logic needs.

findings.topic: RBAC — ModuleName list is a hardcoded TS union; 'checklists' must be added
findings.detail: src/types/rbac.ts:31-63 defines `ModuleName` verbatim as: dashboard, events, performers, customers, employees, messages, sms_health, settings, reports, users, roles, private_bookings, table_bookings, invoices, oj_projects, receipts, loyalty, quotes, parking, short_links, feedback, menu_management, cashing_up, rota, leave, timeclock, payroll, recruitment, mileage, expenses, mgd. Note the convention: multi-word modules are snake_case ('table_bookings', 'cashing_up'), NOT kebab. src/types/rbac.ts:65-105 defines `ActionType` verbatim: view, create, edit, delete, export, manage, send, convert, view_documents, upload_documents, delete_documents, view_templates, manage_templates, manage_roles, view_pricing, manage_deposits, view_vendor_costs, manage_spaces, manage_catering, manage_vendors, generate_contracts, gm_override, view_sensitive, view_sms_queue, approve_sms, enroll, redeem, refund, submit, approve, lock, unlock, publish, request, clock, view_contact_preferences, manage_contact_preferences, manage_whatsapp_opt_in, record_service_contact, send_transactional, send_marketing, view_consent_audit, export_consent_audit.
findings.implication: Adding a `checklists` module = one line in the ModuleName union plus a migration. Existing actions cover everything the feature needs: `view`, `create`/`edit`/`delete` (superadmin setup), `manage` (spot-check recording), `submit` (tick a task). No new ActionType is required unless you want a distinct spot-check action — reuse `manage` and avoid touching the union twice.

findings.topic: RBAC seeding — the migration pattern is well-established, two variants
findings.detail: Permissions live in `permissions(module_name, action, description)` and are joined to roles via `role_permissions(role_id, permission_id)`. Two house patterns exist. (1) Terse INSERT ... ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING — supabase/migrations/20260405130000_mileage_expenses_mgd.sql:402-421: `INSERT INTO public.permissions (module_name, action, description) VALUES ('mileage','view',...), ... ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING;` then `INSERT INTO public.role_permissions (role_id, permission_id) SELECT r.id, p.id FROM public.roles r CROSS JOIN public.permissions p WHERE r.name = 'super_admin' AND p.module_name IN ('mileage','expenses','mgd') ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING;`. (2) Defensive DO $$ block with per-role IF EXISTS guards — supabase/migrations/20260703090000_feedback_rbac_permissions.sql (whole file, 49 lines) is the cleanest end-to-end example: declares super_admin_role_id/manager_role_id, inserts permissions if absent, then back-fills role_permissions for super_admin and manager separately. supabase/migrations/20260707000000_recruitment_foundation.sql:101-143 shows the differentiated-role variant: super_admin gets ALL actions for the module; manager gets only a named subset (`action IN ('view','create','edit','manage','send')`) via a FOR loop.
findings.implication: Copy 20260703090000_feedback_rbac_permissions.sql as the template. For checklists you want three tiers: super_admin = all actions (setup, insights, spot checks); manager = view/submit/manage; staff = view + submit only. Follow the recruitment file's `action IN (...)` subset pattern for the staff role. Note: staff must hold checklists/view or they cannot use the FOH modal at all.

findings.topic: super_admin bypasses permission rows entirely
findings.detail: src/services/permission.ts:180-196 — `PermissionService.checkUserPermission(moduleName, action, userId)` short-circuits `if (await getCachedIsSuperAdmin(userId)) return true;` before looking at any permission rows. Both super-admin status and the permission list are cached via `unstable_cache` for 60s under tag `permissions-${userId}` (src/services/permission.ts:145-177).
findings.implication: Superadmin-only screens (checklist setup, insights, spot-check recording) work even if you seed no rows — but seed them anyway for consistency. Practical gotcha: after any role/permission change there is up to a 60s stale window; do not write tests or manual QA that assume instant permission propagation.

findings.topic: checkUserPermission — signature and the two call sites patterns
findings.detail: Server-action wrapper: src/app/actions/rbac.ts:64-79 — `export async function checkUserPermission(moduleName: ModuleName, action: ActionType, userId?: string): Promise<boolean>`. When `userId` is omitted it resolves the user via `createClient()` + `supabase.auth.getUser()`. PAGE pattern (src/app/(authenticated)/mileage/insights/page.tsx:15-17): `const canView = await checkUserPermission('mileage','view'); if (!canView) redirect('/unauthorized')`. SERVER ACTION pattern (src/app/actions/cashing-up.ts:311-323): resolve user via `createClient()` + `getUser()`, return `{ success:false, error:'Unauthorized' }` if none, then `await PermissionService.checkUserPermission('cashing_up','view', user.id)` → `{ success:false, error:'Forbidden' }`. A third house pattern is a per-module `requirePermission` helper that throws — src/app/actions/rbac.ts:20-38 and `requireMileagePermission('view')` at src/app/actions/mileage.ts:1355.
findings.implication: For checklists: pages use `checkUserPermission(...)` + `redirect('/unauthorized')`; actions use a local `requireChecklistPermission(action)` helper (mileage's is the cleanest model) so every mutation re-checks server-side. Pass `user.id` explicitly in actions where you already resolved the user — the wrapper skips a duplicate getUser() call.

findings.topic: NAV — adding a permission-gated item is a one-line change
findings.detail: src/ds/shell/SidebarNav.tsx:11-26 defines `NavItem { id, label, icon, href, badge?, permission?: { module: ModuleName; action: ActionType } }` and `NavGroup { label: string|null; items: NavItem[] }`. `NAV_GROUPS` (src/ds/shell/SidebarNav.tsx:28-79) is 5 unlabelled groups: (1) dashboard/events/customers/messages/feedback, (2) menu/tables/private-bookings/parking, (3) employees/recruitment/rota, (4) cashing-up/invoices/quotes/oj-projects/receipts/mileage/expenses/mgd/short-links, (5) settings/users/roles/profile. `filterNavGroupsForPermissions` (src/ds/shell/SidebarNav.tsx:81-94) drops items whose permission fails and drops groups that end up empty. `navCount` (src/ds/shell/SidebarNav.tsx:101-125) maps nav id → live badge count from `OutstandingCounts`.
findings.implication: Add `{ id: 'checklists', label: 'Checklists', icon: 'grid', href: '/checklists', permission: { module: 'checklists', action: 'manage' } }` to GROUP 3 (employees/recruitment/rota) — checklists are rota- and people-shaped, and that group is the people-ops cluster. Gate on `manage` rather than `view` so the nav item is superadmin/manager-only while staff still hold `view` for the FOH modal. If you want an overdue-task badge, extend `OutstandingCounts` and add a `checklists:` line to the `countById` map at SidebarNav.tsx:109-118.

findings.topic: REPORTING — house pattern is aggregate-in-TypeScript, not SQL views or RPCs
findings.detail: Ten insights/reporting screens exist: customers/insights, mgd/insights, short-links/insights, table-bookings/reports, cashing-up/insights, cashing-up/dashboard, expenses/insights, rota/dashboard, rota/hours, mileage/insights. Every one aggregates in TS. Best example for your use case: src/app/actions/mileage.ts:1350-1450+ `getMileageInsights(granularity: MileageGranularity = 'monthly')` — permission check, `createAdminClient()`, fetch raw rows, then bucket them in a `Map<string, {...}>` by period key (annually/quarterly/monthly branches at lines 1385-1401), sort by `periodStart.localeCompare`, then a second query joins child rows for the per-dimension breakdown (lines 1419-1450). Page: src/app/(authenticated)/mileage/insights/page.tsx:15-38 — server page does permission check → calls the action → renders `<MileageInsightsClient initialData={result.data} />` inside `PageLayout` with `navItems`, with an `<Alert variant="error">` branch when the fetch fails. cashing-up is the same shape but pushes the aggregation into a service (`CashingUpService.getInsightsData`, called from src/app/actions/cashing-up.ts:334). Views do exist in migrations (e.g. supabase/migrations/20260121130000_project_stats_view.sql) but are NOT the pattern for insights screens.
findings.implication: Build checklist insights as a `getChecklistInsights(granularity)` server action that fetches raw completion rows and buckets them in TS. Mirror the mileage file structure exactly: server page (permission → action → PageLayout+navItems → Client component with initialData) and the two-query pattern (completions bucketed by period, then a second query for the per-person breakdown). Do not invent a SQL view — it would be the only one of its kind and reviewers will flag it.

findings.topic: Per-person stats screen already exists — rota/hours is the model for scoring
findings.detail: src/app/(authenticated)/rota/hours/page.tsx is the closest existing per-employee breakdown: `HoursByEmployeeClient` with typed series `HoursEmployeeOption`, `HolidayEmployeeSummary`, `SickEmployeeSummary`, `HoursSeries`, `WeeklyHoursRow` (imported at lines 8-17); `export const dynamic = 'force-dynamic'` (line 19); a hardcoded `COLOURS` array mixing design tokens with hex (lines 22-31); searchParams `{ from, to, employee }` (lines 34-38); admin client + `calculatePaidHours` from src/lib/rota/pay-calculator. Also src/app/(authenticated)/rota/payroll/ has `payrollCycleStats.ts` + `payrollCycleStats.test.ts` + `PayrollSummaryBar.tsx` — a pure-function stats module with unit tests.
findings.implication: There is NO leaderboard/scoring screen anywhere — checklist scoring is genuinely new UI, but rota/hours gives you the layout (employee multi-select + date range + per-person series) and payrollCycleStats.ts gives you the pattern for a pure, unit-tested scoring function. Put the score calculation in `src/lib/checklists/score.ts` as a pure function with a co-located `.test.ts` — that is the only precedent for testable stats logic and it satisfies the 90% business-logic coverage rule.

findings.topic: Spot checks → employee audit log: the 'audit log' is audit_logs filtered by resource
findings.detail: The employee audit trail is not a dedicated table. src/services/employees.ts:1019 queries `supabase.from('audit_logs').select('*').eq('resource_type','employee').eq('resource_id', employeeId).order('created_at',{ascending:false}).limit(50)`, returned as `auditLogs` (src/services/employees.ts:1042) and rendered by `EmployeeAuditTrail` (src/components/features/employees/EmployeeAuditTrail.tsx:1-40, mounted at src/app/(authenticated)/employees/[employee_id]/page.tsx:445-452), which merges audit entries with `employee_notes` into one timeline. Writer: `logAuditEvent(params)` at src/app/actions/audit.ts:5-7 → `AuditService.logAuditEvent` (src/services/audit.ts:18-60), params `{ user_id, user_email, operation_type, resource_type, resource_id, operation_status, old_values, new_values, additional_info }`.
findings.implication: To land a failed spot check on Billy's employee audit log, call `logAuditEvent({ resource_type: 'employee', resource_id: <employee_id>, operation_type: 'spot_check_fail', operation_status: 'success', additional_info: { taskId, result, notes } })`. The `resource_type:'employee'` + `resource_id:<employee_id>` pair is load-bearing — any other resource_type and the entry will never appear on the employee page.
findings.blocker: Two real constraints. (1) The employee query is hardcoded `.limit(50)` (src/services/employees.ts:1019) — 2 spot checks/day is ~730/year, so checklist entries will flood the trail and push real HR history off the page within weeks. Decide: raise/paginate the limit, or filter spot-checks out of the default view. (2) `audit_logs.user_id` is the ACTING user (Billy), not the person being checked — the credited employee must go in `resource_id`/`additional_info`, not `user_id`.

findings.topic: Attribution — clocked-in staff list already exists and FOH already uses it
findings.detail: src/app/actions/timeclock.ts:314-339 `getOpenSessions()` returns `{ success: true; data: (TimeclockSession & { employee_name: string })[] }` — queries `timeclock_sessions` where `clock_out_at IS NULL`, embeds `employees!timeclock_sessions_employee_id_fkey(first_name, last_name)`, orders by `clock_in_at`. The FOH page already imports it (src/app/(authenticated)/table-bookings/foh/page.tsx:9). src/app/actions/timeclock.ts:346-354 `linkSessionToShift` links a timeclock session to its scheduled `rota_shifts` row (same work_date + employee, clock-in within 2 hours of shift start).
findings.implication: The 'show everyone currently clocked in' attribution picker is free — call `getOpenSessions()`. Credit rows should store `employee_id` (from the session), so a checklist_task_completions ↔ checklist_completion_credits (many-to-many) shape falls out naturally. `linkSessionToShift` gives you the session→shift link needed to answer 'who was on and did not do it'.

findings.topic: Rota shifts are already tagged bar/kitchen — matches the task tagging exactly
findings.detail: supabase/migrations/20260228100000_rota_system.sql:36 and :59 — both the shift template and `rota_shifts` carry `department TEXT NOT NULL CHECK (department IN ('bar','kitchen'))`, indexed at line 81 (`idx_rota_shifts_department`). `department_budgets` uses the same CHECK at line 239.
findings.implication: Use the identical `department TEXT NOT NULL CHECK (department IN ('bar','kitchen'))` column on checklist tasks — not a new enum, not 'BAR'/'KITCHEN' casing. This makes the rota join for 'who was rostered on the bar today' a direct equality, and 'the rota is the source of truth for opening hours' resolves to querying rota_shifts for the day rather than business_hours.

findings.topic: FOH-only users see no sidebar — the checklists nav item is invisible to them by design
findings.detail: src/lib/foh/user-mode.ts:7-21 — `isFohOnlyUser(permissions)` returns true only when the user has `table_bookings/view` AND `permissions.every(p => p.module_name === 'table_bookings')`. The FOH page uses it to strip navItems and the back button (src/app/(authenticated)/table-bookings/foh/page.tsx:53-60). There is also a hardcoded `const MANAGER_IPAD_EMAIL = 'manager@the-anchor.pub'` at line 14.
findings.implication: The FOH modal must be reachable from the FOH page itself, never via nav — which matches the owner's decision. Superadmin insights nav is unaffected.
findings.blocker: Granting FOH staff a `checklists/view` permission BREAKS `isFohOnlyUser` — the `.every(p => p.module_name === 'table_bookings')` test fails the moment a second module appears, and those users would suddenly get the full sidebar chrome on the FOH iPad. Decide before building: either (a) extend isFohOnlyUser to allow a `checklists` module alongside table_bookings (one-line change, my recommendation), or (b) gate the FOH checklist modal on `table_bookings/view` and skip a checklists permission for staff entirely.

findings.topic: Manager alert email: the actual rota template and its send path
findings.detail: buildManagerAlertEmailHtml is at src/lib/rota/email-templates.ts:266-289. It is a pure HTML-string builder taking only (weekStart, reason) — no recipient, no logging, no send. Its ONLY caller is the cron at src/app/api/cron/rota-manager-alert/route.ts:65. That route is the whole pattern in one file: authorizeCronRequest (line 20) → London wall-time gate (line 27-31) → query state → resolve recipient from system_settings key 'rota_manager_email' falling back to process.env.ROTA_MANAGER_EMAIL (lines 50-58) → skip silently if no recipient (line 59) → sendEmail({to, subject, html}) (line 61) → INSERT into rota_email_log with status 'sent'|'failed' + error_message (lines 67-76). Note the DB-setting-then-env precedence: settings UI wins over env. Template internals worth copying: APP_URL from process.env.NEXT_PUBLIC_APP_URL at module top (line 3), escapeHtml() helper (line 58), and dates rendered with date-fns format(parseISO(...)) — a DATE-ONLY string, never an instant.
findings.implication: Copy this exact shape for checklist emails: a pure builder in a lib/checklists/email-templates.ts, and a route that gates, resolves recipient, sends, then logs. Do NOT put sending inside the builder. Reuse escapeHtml for the free-text NOTES box and for staff names — notes are staff-typed and go straight into an inbox.

findings.topic: Lesson from baa8f428 — what must NOT be repeated
findings.detail: Two defects, one commit. (1) `new Date(appointment.scheduled_start).toLocaleString('en-GB')` at src/app/api/recruitment/booking/[token]/route.ts:99 and .../reschedule/route.ts:58 rendered a stored UTC instant in the SERVER's zone — right on a London laptop, an hour early on Vercel (UTC). Replaced with formatRecruitmentAppointmentTime() (src/services/recruitment.ts). (2) The alert named nobody: 'Interview booked for 21/07/2026, 14:00:00 at The Anchor.' Fixed by describeRecruitmentAppointmentCandidate() (src/lib/recruitment/communications.ts:370-380), which falls back name → email → 'A candidate' and never renders empty. Regression test src/lib/recruitment/__tests__/manager-alert.test.ts pins 14:00Z == 15:00 Europe/London. The commit message states the reasoning: 'Alerts are read in an inbox, away from the ATS, so both halves matter: who it is about, and a time that matches what the candidate was told.'
findings.implication: Both defects apply directly to the threshold-breach email. NEVER format an instant with toLocaleString/new Date() — use formatInTimeZone/Intl with Europe/London explicitly. And the email body must name WHO recorded the value, WHICH task, the value, the threshold, and the London time — an alert saying 'Cellar cooler out of range' is unactionable from an inbox. Any test asserting a time must use a BST instant (e.g. 14:00Z→15:00) or it passes on a London laptop while prod is wrong.

findings.topic: rota_email_log schema and the email_type CHECK — recommendation: own table
findings.detail: Table created at supabase/migrations/20260228100000_rota_system.sql:272-299: id, email_type (CHECK), entity_type TEXT ('leave_request'|'rota_week'|'payroll_month'), entity_id UUID, to_addresses TEXT[] NOT NULL, cc_addresses TEXT[], subject TEXT NOT NULL, status TEXT CHECK IN ('sent','failed'), error_message, message_id, sent_by UUID→auth.users (NULL = automated), sent_at, created_at. Indexes on email_type, (entity_type, entity_id), sent_at DESC. RLS on (line 372) with a blanket 'Authenticated users can manage rota_email_log' FOR ALL policy (line 429). The CHECK has already been rewritten once — supabase/migrations/20260704000000_portal_shift_acceptance.sql:252-281 drops the constraint by looking it up in pg_constraint (the original was unnamed) and re-adds it as rota_email_log_email_type_check with 10 values.
findings.implication: RECOMMEND a separate checklist_email_log rather than extending the CHECK. Three reasons: (a) COMMENT ON TABLE at line 295 scopes it to 'rota-related outgoing emails' — checklist emails are not rota emails even though they read rota shifts; (b) entity_type is a loose TEXT tag with no FK, so pointing it at checklist_task_instances gives no integrity and pollutes an audited rota surface; (c) the blanket authenticated FOR ALL policy is wrong for a superadmin-only insights table. Copy the column shape verbatim (to_addresses TEXT[], status CHECK, message_id, sent_by NULL=automated) — it is proven — and use a proper FK plus a restrictive RLS policy. If you DO extend the CHECK instead, you must follow the 20260704 drop-by-lookup pattern, not a bare DROP CONSTRAINT by name.
findings.blocker: Decision needed: new checklist_email_log (recommended) vs extending rota_email_log's CHECK.

findings.topic: Weekly cron precedent — and a live DST bug in the one that looks like the precedent
findings.detail: The obvious weekly cron is `0 18 * * 0 /api/cron/rota-manager-alert` (vercel.json). Its route comment at src/app/api/cron/rota-manager-alert/route.ts:8 claims 'runs at 18:00 Europe/London every Sunday' — that is FALSE. Vercel crons are UTC, so it fires 19:00 London during BST and 18:00 only in GMT. The route re-checks the day only (`nowLocal.getDay() !== 0`, line 28) via toZonedTime — it never re-checks the HOUR. It drifts an hour twice a year and nothing catches it. The CORRECT precedent is src/app/api/cron/private-bookings-weekly-summary/route.ts, scheduled `0 * * * *` (EVERY HOUR) and gating in-route: getLondonDateParts() (line 38-64) uses Intl.DateTimeFormat with timeZone 'Europe/London', hour12:false, formatToParts to extract {dateKey, hour, dayOfWeek}, then compares hour against parseDigestHour() (line 80, env PRIVATE_BOOKINGS_WEEKLY_DIGEST_HOUR_LONDON, default 9) and dayOfWeek. It also wraps the run in claimIdempotencyKey/persistIdempotencyResponse (imports lines 5-10) so the 24 hourly invocations can't double-send.
findings.implication: Use the private-bookings-weekly-summary pattern for the weekly checklist summary: schedule `0 * * * *`, gate on London weekday+hour in-route, and claim an idempotency key on the London dateKey so only one of the 24 hourly hits sends. Do NOT copy rota-manager-alert's `0 18 * * 0` + day-only gate — it is the bug, not the pattern. Note getLondonDateParts is private to that route file; lift it into a shared helper rather than a third copy.

findings.topic: Every-2-hours-during-trading — use the jobs queue, not a new cron
findings.detail: There IS a queue and it is the right tool. Cron `* * * * * /api/jobs/process?process=true&batch=30` (vercel.json, first entry). Route src/app/api/jobs/process/route.ts: GET and POST both authorizeCronRequest then jobQueue.processJobs(batchSize) — batch clamped 1..100, default 30 (lines 22-26); `?process=false` or `?health=true` makes it a health check (lines 12-18); maxDuration 60. Table `jobs` (supabase/migrations/20251123120000_squashed.sql:9848-9875): id, type VARCHAR(50), payload JSONB, status CHECK IN ('pending','processing','completed','failed','cancelled'), attempts, max_attempts DEFAULT 3, scheduled_for TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT NOW(), started_at/completed_at/failed_at, error_message, result JSONB, priority INTEGER DEFAULT 0, created_at, updated_at. RLS on, service_role only (line 9869). Leasing added by supabase/migrations/20260414000000_job_queue_claiming.sql: processing_token uuid, lease_expires_at, last_heartbeat_at, plus claim_jobs(batch_size, job_types text[], lease_seconds) — SECURITY DEFINER, FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED, ORDER BY priority DESC, created_at ASC, WHERE status='pending' AND scheduled_for <= NOW(), granted to service_role only. API: jobQueue.enqueue(type, payload, {priority, maxAttempts, delay (ms), unique}) at src/lib/unified-job-queue.ts:212; `delay` sets scheduled_for = now+delay (line 351); `unique` takes an idempotency lock (buildJobEnqueueLockKey, 15-min TTL) AND is copied into payload.unique_key (line 347). Execution heartbeats the lease every 30s and aborts if the token is stolen (processJob, line 753+).
findings.implication: RECOMMEND: no new cron for the 2-hourly tasks. Generate the day's task instances once (from a daily/hourly gated cron reading the ROTA for that day's real open/close times) and enqueue each 2-hourly instance with `delay` set to its London due-instant, `unique` set to `checklist:<templateId>:<londonDateKey>:<slot>` for free de-duplication. scheduled_for solves 'trading hours vary' outright — the rota decides the instants at generation time, so no cron ever needs to know when the pub opens. IMPORTANT gotchas: (1) a new job type must be added in THREE places or it silently never runs or hard-fails — the JobType union (line 26-41), the SUPPORTED_JOB_TYPES array (line 43-59, which filters claim_jobs at line 642), and the executeJob switch (line 985), whose default throws `Unknown job type: ${type}` (~line 1225). (2) The queue is service_role-only, so anything reading queue state for the superadmin insights screen needs a server action, not a client query.

findings.topic: manager@the-anchor.pub is hard-coded in at least 5 live send paths
findings.detail: It IS a live recipient today, and inconsistently configured. Hard-coded constants with NO env override: src/app/actions/rota.ts:175 (MANAGER_SHIFT_EMAIL), src/app/api/cron/rota-shift-acceptance/route.ts:13 (MANAGER_SHIFT_EMAIL), src/lib/table-bookings/bookings.ts:134 (MANAGER_TABLE_BOOKING_EMAIL), src/app/api/external/performer-interest/route.ts:280 (inline `to:`), src/app/(authenticated)/table-bookings/foh/page.tsx:14 and src/app/actions/timeclock.ts:21 (MANAGER_IPAD_EMAIL). Env-var-with-fallback (the good pattern): src/app/actions/employee-birthdays.ts:107 and src/app/api/feedback/route.ts:20 use `process.env.MANAGER_EMAIL || 'manager@the-anchor.pub'`; src/lib/recruitment/communications.ts:395,989 use RECRUITMENT_NOTIFICATION_EMAIL||fallback; src/lib/table-bookings/charge-approvals.ts:8 uses MANAGER_APPROVAL_EMAIL||fallback; src/app/api/cron/private-bookings-weekly-summary/route.ts:149 uses PRIVATE_BOOKINGS_MANAGER_EMAIL||fallback. The richest pattern is rota-manager-alert (route.ts:50-58): system_settings row 'rota_manager_email' FIRST, then process.env.ROTA_MANAGER_EMAIL, then skip. .env.example documents ROTA_MANAGER_EMAIL (line 139), PAYROLL_ACCOUNTANT_EMAIL (line 140), OJ_PROJECTS_BILLING_ALERT_EMAIL (141, 'falls back to PAYROLL_ACCOUNTANT_EMAIL'), MANAGER_EMAIL (144), PRIVATE_BOOKINGS_MANAGER_EMAIL (50).
findings.implication: YES — make it an env var: `CHECKLIST_MANAGER_EMAIL=manager@the-anchor.pub` in .env.example under a new 'Checklists' heading, read as `process.env.CHECKLIST_MANAGER_EMAIL || 'manager@the-anchor.pub'`. Do NOT reuse MANAGER_EMAIL — .env.example:144 already scopes it to birthdays/onboarding/feedback, so overloading it silently redirects three unrelated flows. Do not hard-code a bare constant; that's the pattern the codebase is drifting away from. Since the owner wants a superadmin setup screen anyway, the rota-manager-alert precedent (system_settings → env → skip) is worth matching — but env-only is acceptable for v1 and can be layered later.

findings.topic: Threshold-breach email must not silently no-op
findings.detail: rota-manager-alert returns `{ok:true, action:'skipped_no_email'}` when no recipient resolves (route.ts:59-60) — a 200, invisible in Vercel logs. src/lib/oj-projects/billing-alerts.ts:63-67 does the same but at least console.warns 'No alert email configured (...); skipping.' The queue's counterpart failure mode is louder: executeJob's default throws, and a thrown job retries to max_attempts then lands in `jobs` with status 'failed' + error_message.
