findings.implication: For the immediate out-of-threshold email, silence is the worst outcome — the staff member has been told on screen to contact Billy, so the record must exist. Log the send attempt to checklist_email_log with status 'failed' BEFORE returning on a missing/failed recipient, and console.error rather than returning a bare ok. The value reading itself must be persisted independently of the email — never let a failed send roll back or block the tick.

verified.claim: The rota CANNOT be the source of truth for the actual trading day — prod data disproves it

verdict.topic: Rota as source of truth for the trading day — adversarial verification
verdict.detail:
CONFIRMED (all sub-claims independently reproduced against prod tfcasgxopxegwrabvwat; I tried to refute each and could not).

1) Disagreement in BOTH directions — confirmed exactly as stated:
- 2026-04-03 (Good Friday): special_hours.opens=16:00, closes=22:00, note='Good Friday'. Earliest rota_shifts row = 14:00–18:00 bar 'Friday Open' (status='scheduled'). Rota is 2h EARLIER.
- 2026-05-25 (Spring bank holiday): special_hours.opens=12:00, note='Spring bank holiday! Kitchen and bar open from 12pm!'. The ONLY rota row for that date = 16:00–22:00 bar 'Monday' (status='scheduled'). Rota is 4h LATER — nobody rostered for the advertised 12:00 open. This is the fatal direction: the rota does not merely add pre-open prep, it silently MISSES an advertised opening. A checklist system keyed on the rota would have generated no open checklist until 16:00 on a day the pub advertised trading from 12:00.
I checked `status` specifically to rule out the refutation that these were cancelled/sick rows — both are 'scheduled', and `is_open_shift=false`. Not an artefact.

2) Normal weeks (2026-07-06→2026-07-26, status='scheduled') — confirmed, with one nuance that does NOT rescue the rota:
- Mon–Fri: earliest shift == business_hours.opens (16:00) on every day.
- Sundays 07-12/07-19/07-26: earliest = 10:30 'Cleaning', 90min before the 12:00 open — so min(start_time) is NOT the open time.
- Sat 07-11 and 07-18: max(end_time)=21:00 vs 22:00 close — no bar-close shift rostered. Sat 07-25 does reach 22:00, so this is inconsistent week to week, not a stable rule.
- Nuance I probed as a possible refutation: the rota DOES contain shifts literally named 'Sunday Open'/'Saturday Open' starting exactly at business_hours.opens (12:00). So a naming convention nearly encodes the open time. But `name` is free text (rota_shifts.name is `text`), the convention is not enforced anywhere, and it BREAKS on 2026-05-25 (the sole shift is named 'Monday', no 'Open' shift at all). So this does not make the rota derivable.

3) 'What the app already does' — confirmed in code, and it is the very page this feature attaches to:
- src/app/api/foh/schedule/route.ts:251-254 loads special_hours by date; :303-306 resolves `specialHours?.opens ?? businessHours?.opens` (same for closes/kitchen_opens/kitchen_closes), and :293-302 resolves is_closed/is_kitchen_closed with special_hours taking precedence. That is the special_hours→business_hours precedence chain, already live on the FOH schedule the checklists modal is specced to sit on.
- Same precedence pattern in src/services/business-hours.ts and src/app/api/business/hours/route.ts (public hours API), so it is the app-wide convention, not a one-off.
- Schema verified live (information_schema.columns on rota_shifts): there is NO column recording actual/intended open or close — only shift_date/start_time/end_time/department/status/name. The rota structurally cannot express 'the pub opens at 12:00 today'; it only expresses 'this person works 16:00–22:00'.
verdict.implication:
The spec's stated rule 'THE ROTA is the source of truth for whether the pub opens later/earlier than normal — NOT the business_hours table' is false as written and must be rewritten before build. Two distinct concepts are conflated and need separating in the data model:
- EXPECTED trading day (when should the open checklist be due? when do the every-2h mid-shift tasks start and stop?) → resolve special_hours→business_hours, reusing the exact precedence already at route.ts:303-306. Do not re-implement it; extract or reuse it so FOH and checklists cannot drift apart.
- WHO is accountable (who was on, who closed, who did not do it) → the rota. This is what rota_shifts genuinely models, and the BAR/KITCHEN tagging maps cleanly onto rota_shifts.department (values 'bar'/'kitchen' confirmed in prod).
- ACTUAL open/close → nothing records this today. The open checklist's own completion timestamp is the natural place to capture it, which is option (b) and is cheap because the feature is being built anyway.
Concrete consequences if this is not resolved: on 2026-05-25 no open checklist fires until 16:00 against a 12:00 advertised open; on every Sunday the 10:30 'Cleaning' shift would drag the open checklist 90min early; on Saturdays with no close shift (07-11, 07-18) the close checklist would either never fire or attach to the wrong person — and the spec makes the closer responsible for ALL checks, so mis-resolving the close shift mis-assigns accountability and corrupts the per-person scoring the superadmin sees.
The reviewer's recommendation (a)+(b) is sound and I endorse it on the evidence: derive EXPECTED from special_hours→business_hours, use the rota only for WHO, and let the checklist record the ACTUAL. Note this also makes the rota-vs-hours gap VISIBLE (e.g. an open checklist due at 12:00 with nobody rostered until 16:00 is exactly the 2026-05-25 failure surfacing itself), which is a feature worth keeping rather than designing around.
verdict.blocker: YES — survives verification, unchanged. The spec's load-bearing assumption is false and prod data disproves it in both directions. Owner decision needed before schema/build: (a) resolve the EXPECTED trading day from special_hours→business_hours (reusing the existing precedence at src/app/api/foh/schedule/route.ts:303-306) and use the rota ONLY for WHO is accountable; plus (b) let the open/close checklist's own completion timestamp become the ACTUAL open/close record. Recommend (a)+(b) — it costs no new source of truth, matches what the FOH page the feature attaches to already does, and (b) is near-free since the checklist is being built regardless. If the owner insists the rota drives timing, they must first accept that 2026-05-25-style days silently produce no open checklist, and that Sunday's 10:30 'Cleaning' shift fires the open checklist 90min early — neither is fixable in code without a new explicit open/close field on the rota.

verified.claim: Something already reconciles trading hours — and it does not read the rota

verdict.topic: FOH trading-hours resolution reads business_hours + special_hours, never the rota
verdict.detail: PARTIAL — the code claim is CONFIRMED exactly; the supporting data claim is overstated. CONFIRMED: src/app/api/foh/schedule/route.ts:241-258 fetches `business_hours` (by day_of_week, line 247-250) and `special_hours` (by date, line 251-254) in the same Promise.all. Lines 290-321 resolve the day: `specialHours ? specialHours.is_closed : businessHours?.is_closed` (293-297), `normalizeClock(specialHours?.opens ?? businessHours?.opens ?? null)` (303) and the same coalesce for closes/kitchen_opens/kitchen_closes (304-306), producing a ServiceWindow tagged `source: 'fallback' | 'business_hours'` (route.ts:153, 285, 318) with a hardcoded 09:00-23:00 fallback (277-286). The rota is consulted nowhere: `grep -rn 'rota' src/app/api/foh/ src/lib/foh/` returns zero hits, and `grep -rln 'rota' 'src/app/(authenticated)/table-bookings/foh/'` returns zero files. The resolved window is live-consumed by the FOH page — src/app/(authenticated)/table-bookings/foh/utils.ts:196-199 (grid extent) and 456-499 (pub/kitchen open-closed bands). REFUTED in detail: special_hours has 62 rows total, but only 8 (not 12) are dated within the last 3 months, and only 8 were created in the last 3 months (max_date 2026-07-05, last_edit 2026-07-05 15:11 UTC, future_rows = 0 as of 2026-07-17). The named overrides do exist and are real (2026-04-06 Easter Monday opens 14:00 kitchen closed; 2026-05-04 May Day opens 16:00; 2026-05-07 opens 16:00 kitchen closed; 2026-07-05 opens 12:00 kitchen 13:00-16:00). So 'live and maintained' holds, but 'actively maintained' is weaker than stated — nothing is forward-dated, and the table has been untouched for 12 days.
verdict.implication: The checklists build cannot treat 'the rota is the source of truth for trading hours' as a greenfield decision. A working, in-production resolver already answers 'when does the pub open/close today' from business_hours + special_hours, and the FOH page — the exact screen the checklists modal will live on — renders its grid and its open/closed bands from that answer. Building open/close checklist scheduling off rota_shifts instead creates two disagreeing sources of trading truth on one screen: the FOH grid would show 12:00-22:00 from special_hours while the close checklist fired off a rota shift ending 23:00. Either the checklists reuse the existing resolver, or the resolver is changed to consult the rota (which changes FOH grid behaviour for every user and needs regression cover), or the owner accepts two sources. That is a design fork, not an implementation detail. Note also that special_hours currently has no forward-dated rows — so on any given future day the resolver silently falls back to the weekly business_hours pattern, which is arguably the practical reason the owner reached for the rota in the first place.
verdict.blocker: Not a hard blocker — nothing prevents building. But it is a decision only the owner can make, and it must be put to them explicitly before checklist scheduling is designed: the 'rota not business_hours' instruction directly contradicts a working, in-production mechanism that already drives the same FOH screen. Ask: (a) should the checklists reuse the existing business_hours/special_hours resolver, (b) should the resolver itself be changed to read the rota (accepting FOH grid regression risk), or (c) do the two coexist? Frame it with the real cause: special_hours is accurate when someone remembers to fill it in, and right now nobody has filled in anything beyond 2026-07-05.

verified.claim: rota_shifts vs rota_published_shifts — which to read

verdict.topic: rota_shifts vs rota_published_shifts — which table the checklists feature should read
verdict.detail: PARTIAL — the substance is CONFIRMED, one stated fact is REFUTED. Confirmed: rota_shifts is the manager draft (supabase/migrations/20260228100000_rota_system.sql:50-73). rota_published_shifts is the snapshot and its PK is explicitly the same UUID as the rota_shifts row (20260503000002_published_shifts_snapshot.sql:5-6, comment "same UUID as rota_shifts.id"), with read RLS open to all authenticated users (same file, lines 32-38). Publish is delete-then-insert per week via the admin client (src/app/actions/rota.ts:2886 createAdminClient(), :2994-3005 delete .eq('week_id') then insert), with the comment at rota.ts:2884 confirming the snapshot table has no write RLS for regular users; rota_weeks.status flips to 'published' only after the snapshot succeeds (rota.ts:3007-3014). Cancelled rows are excluded at publish time (rota.ts:2878 `.neq('status', 'cancelled')`). REFUTED: the column count. Live prod information_schema gives rota_shifts 34 columns, not 30 (the claim's list omits acceptance_status/acceptance_decided_at/acceptance_decided_by/acceptance_note/auto_accept_reason/auto_accept_warning_sent_at being present on BOTH — miscounted). rota_published_shifts is 25 columns, which IS correct. The delta is also slightly understated: published drops template_id, sick_reason, original_employee_id, reassigned_from_id, reassigned_at, reassigned_by, reassignment_reason, created_by AND created_at AND updated_at (9 cols per the claim, actually 10 — created_at/updated_at were not mentioned), and adds published_at. Every column the claim says both tables carry, they do carry — verified against prod, not just migrations. Reader-side evidence corroborates the recommendation: staff-facing surfaces already read the snapshot (src/app/(staff-portal)/portal/shifts/page.tsx, src/app/api/portal/calendar-feed/route.ts, src/app/api/rota/feed/route.ts, src/app/(authenticated)/rota/page.tsx).
verdict.implication: Read rota_published_shifts for checklists. It carries everything the feature needs — employee_id, shift_date, start_time, end_time, department ('bar'/'kitchen', matching the BAR/KITCHEN task tags), status and is_overnight — so shift-tied open/close attribution and "who did not do it" work off the snapshot exactly as the staff portal already does. Its permissive SELECT RLS for authenticated users means no new policy work. Two behaviours to design around, both confirmed rather than speculative: (1) status='cancelled' rows never reach the snapshot (rota.ts:2878), so a cancelled shift is simply absent — do not expect to filter it out yourself; (2) the id is shared with rota_shifts, so a checklist FK can point at either, but pointing at rota_published_shifts risks dangling on delete-then-insert republish (the row is deleted and re-inserted with the same id, and rota_published_shifts has no ON DELETE behaviour protecting dependants). Store employee_id + shift_date + department on the checklist row rather than FK-ing the snapshot id. Do not use the claim's "30 columns" figure in any schema doc — it is wrong; rota_shifts has 34. Also note created_at/updated_at are absent from the snapshot, so published rows carry no per-row mutation timestamp other than published_at (uniform across the week).

verified.claim: is_overnight exists but is a display/pay flag, not a trading-day boundary

verdict.topic: rota_shifts.is_overnight — display/pay flag, not a trading-day boundary
verdict.detail:
PARTIAL. The core claim CONFIRMED, one supporting detail REFUTED.

CONFIRMED — schema: `is_overnight BOOLEAN NOT NULL DEFAULT FALSE` at supabase/migrations/20260228100000_rota_system.sql:62, comment 'TRUE when end_time is on the following calendar day.' at :75. Mirrored on rota_published_shifts (20260503000002_published_shifts_snapshot.sql:16) and on both shadow tables in 20260704000000_portal_shift_acceptance.sql:159,191.

CONFIRMED — NOT derived from end_time. The only triggers on rota_shifts are updated_at (20260228100000_rota_system.sql:320-348); there is no BEFORE INSERT/UPDATE derivation. It is set by a manual checkbox: state at src/app/(authenticated)/rota/ShiftDetailModal.tsx:185, written at :227, and passed through from the Zod field `isOvernight` at src/app/actions/rota.ts:1223. The codebase itself treats the stored value as unreliable — src/lib/google-calendar-rota.ts:387-388 comments 'the shift crosses midnight even if is_overnight wasn't set in the DB' and computes `shift.is_overnight || shift.end_time <= shift.start_time`; src/lib/rota/pay-calculator.ts:99 and :573 apply the same `|| endMinutes < startMinutes` fallback; the SQL at 20260708000013_open_shift_request_manager_approval.sql:123,130,149,168 repeats `rs.is_overnight OR rs.end_time <= rs.start_time`.

CONFIRMED — it is per-employee-shift, not a venue trading-day marker. Nothing reads it to determine when the pub's day starts or ends.

REFUTED — 'Usage is confined to paid-hours maths and a (+1) suffix in the UI'. It is materially wider, and some uses ARE date-boundary semantics: src/lib/ics/utils.ts:46-48 picks the calendar END DATE of the exported VEVENT from it; src/lib/google-calendar-rota.ts:388 does the same for Google Calendar sync; src/lib/rota/publish-status.ts:41 compares it to detect published-vs-draft drift; 20260708000013_open_shift_request_manager_approval.sql:123-168 uses it to build tsranges for employee shift-overlap detection and to widen the sick-marker and approved-leave date checks to shift_date + 1; 20260727000000_premium_rates.sql:12 states premium time windows are 'interpreted on shift_date honouring is_overnight'.

NOT VERIFIED — the prod row counts (129 of 1516, 8.5%; zero in 2026-07-06..07-26) were not checked; no database query was run in this pass.
verdict.implication: Safe to build on the claim's conclusion: do NOT use is_overnight as the checklists trading-day boundary. It is a manually-ticked, per-shift, unreliable flag — an overnight close shift can exist with is_overnight = FALSE. If checklists code needs to know that a close shift lands on the next calendar day (e.g. to attribute a close list to the correct trading day, or to decide 'who was on the closing shift'), it must use the same defensive pattern the rest of the codebase already uses: `is_overnight || end_time <= start_time`. Reading the column bare will silently mis-attribute the ~8.5% of shifts that cross midnight but were never ticked. Also note the claim's 'usage is confined to pay + UI' understates the blast radius — the column already feeds ICS feeds, Google Calendar sync, publish-drift detection, premium-rate windows, and SQL overlap/leave collision checks, so it must not be repurposed or backfilled as part of checklists work.

verified.claim: Shift template naming is NOT reliably Day-Open / Day-Close — two live bar days have no Open/Close split at all

verdict.topic: Shift template naming cannot identify Open/Close — Monday and Wednesday bar are single all-day shifts
verdict.detail:
CONFIRMED (one sub-detail corrected, and additional evidence found that strengthens the claim).

Live prod (Supabase project tfcasgxopxegwrabvwat, `rota_shift_templates`) matches the claimed inventory exactly, template-for-template and time-for-time. All 12 active BAR templates, 8 KITCHEN, 1 CLEANING, 1 HOST, 3 RUNNER are as stated.

Core claim CONFIRMED: bar Monday (id 26f9fe6d, day_of_week=0, 16:00–22:00) and bar Wednesday (id 1b6d1dba, day_of_week=2, 16:00–22:00) are single ACTIVE templates whose names contain neither 'Open' nor 'Close'. Every other active bar day has an explicit Open/Close pair. So on Mon/Wed one person covers open through close and no name-based rule can identify a closer.

CONFIRMED: the merge history. Inactive bar rows exist for 'Monday Open' (b7de100d, 16:00–18:00), 'Monday Close' (ef32042d, 18:00–22:00), 'Wednesday Open' (43c537b3, 16:00–18:00), 'Wednesday Close' (de526063, 18:00–22:00) — these days used to be split and were merged into single templates.

CONFIRMED: duplicate names distinguished only by is_active — 'Friday Close' (d856f61a active / 5cbd14ec inactive), 'Saturday Close' (e61e0e12 / 6ab52734), 'Sunday Kitchen' (e9c0c534 / 7b59caca), 'Tuesday Kitchen' (e92e5b54 / 03138e5f), 'Thursday Kitchen' (9b4e24e5 / 4476ff12), 'Friday Kitchen' (8175da62 / caccff41). Name is not a unique key; any lookup by name must filter is_active.

CORRECTION to one sub-detail: the claim says there are 'inactive Monday/Wednesday/Thursday Open+Close pairs'. There is NO inactive bar Thursday Open/Close pair — bar 'Thursday Open' (66ae11d0, 16:00–18:00) and 'Thursday Close' (982aeee8, 18:00–22:00) are both ACTIVE. The only inactive Thursday row of that shape is 'Thursday Kitchen Open' (ba6991fa). This does not affect the conclusion; only Monday and Wednesday lack an Open/Close split.

CONFIRMED: denormalisation. /Users/peterpitcher/Cursor/OJ-AnchorManagementTools/supabase/migrations/20260503000000_rota_shift_name.sql:1-3 adds `name TEXT` to `rota_shifts` as a free-text column copied from the template — no FK, no constraint, so it drifts.

CONFIRMED and WORSE than stated: prod `rota_shifts` name counts since 2026-05-01 are 'Saturday Close' 19, 'Sunday Close' 18, 'Sunday Open' 18, 'Friday Open' 18, 'Friday Close' 18, 'Saturday Open' 18, 'Monday' 17, 'Wednesday' 17, 'Thursday Open' 17, 'Thursday Close' 17, NULL 13, "Couldn't Work" 15 — all as claimed. But there are ALSO ad-hoc names the claim did not mention: 'Bar' 2, 'Kitchen' 2, 'Runner' 1, 'kitchen' 1 (lower-case), 'Cash Bingo' 1. So the free-text field carries hand-typed, inconsistently-cased values as well as NULLs.

Schema support for the recommended fix is present: `rota_shifts` has `shift_date` (date), `start_time`/`end_time` (time), `department` (text), `status` (text), `is_overnight` (boolean), `template_id` (uuid).
verdict.implication: The checklists build must NOT parse `rota_shifts.name` or `rota_shift_templates.name` to decide who opens or closes — it is free text, nullable (13 rows), duplicated across is_active, hand-typed in places, and structurally absent on Mon/Wed. Closer identification must be computed from `rota_shifts` times/department/status. The proposed rule (latest `end_time` on the date among non-cancelled/non-sick shifts, tie-broken to department='bar') resolves Mon/Wed correctly — the single 16:00–22:00 bar shift is both opener and closer — but note active bar templates all end 22:00, so on split days Close and Kitchen shifts can tie on end_time, making the department='bar' tie-break load-bearing rather than cosmetic. Also filter `status` explicitly: "Couldn't Work" absences are rota_shifts rows (status='sick'), 15 of them since 2026-05-01, and would otherwise be picked as candidate closers.
verdict.blocker: YES (design-level, survives). 'The person CLOSING is responsible for all checks' has no reliable data definition today. Monday and Wednesday have one all-day bar shift with no Open/Close split, and the name field is unreliable everywhere (NULLs, duplicates, ad-hoc values). Owner must confirm the derived rule before build: closer = the non-cancelled, non-sick `rota_shifts` row for that date with the latest `end_time`, tie-broken by `department='bar'`, with a manual override on the close screen. Needs an explicit tie-break decision because active bar Close and Kitchen shifts frequently share an end_time, and a decision on the 13 NULL-name / ad-hoc-name rows.

verified.claim: Departments: FIVE live, not two — and 'tagged BAR or KITCHEN' will orphan 126 shifts

verdict.topic: Departments: five live, not two — 'tagged BAR or KITCHEN' orphans exactly 126 rota shifts
verdict.detail:
CONFIRMED in full — every element verified against live prod (Supabase project tfcasgxopxegwrabvwat) and the migration files; I could not refute any part of it.

1) FIVE live departments, exact match to the claim. `SELECT name, label, sort_order FROM departments ORDER BY sort_order` returns bar/Bar/0, kitchen/Kitchen/1, runner/Runner/2, host/Host/3, cleaning/Cleaning/4. The seed migration supabase/migrations/20260301000001_departments_table.sql:10-14 only inserts bar, kitchen, runner — host and cleaning were added to the live table post-migration, so the repo alone understates the count. `departments` is a real table with `name TEXT PRIMARY KEY` (20260301000001_departments_table.sql:2-7), so it is FK-able today.

2) Prod rota_shifts department counts match to the row: bar 1113, kitchen 277, runner 91, cleaning 18, host 17.

3) The 126 figure is exact, not estimated. `SELECT count(*) FROM rota_shifts WHERE department NOT IN ('bar','kitchen')` returns exactly 126 (= 91 + 18 + 17).

4) Constraint history confirmed exactly as stated. 20260301120000_rota_shifts_drop_department_check.sql:5-6 drops rota_shifts_department_check; 20260306000001_rota_department_constraint.sql:3 carries the corroborating comment ("rota_shifts has no department check constraint (dropped in 20260301120000)") and :8-10 widens rota_shift_templates to ('bar','kitchen','runner'); 20260315000001_rota_shift_templates_drop_department_check.sql:8-9 then drops that check entirely — which is indeed how live 'host' and 'cleaning' templates can exist (3 distinct template departments outside bar/kitchen). rota_published_shifts.department is plain `TEXT NOT NULL` at 20260503000002_published_shifts_snapshot.sql:13.

5) Live pg_constraint confirms the drops actually took effect in prod, not just in the migration files: querying pg_constraint for contype IN ('c','f') across rota_shifts, rota_shift_templates and rota_published_shifts returns NO check and NO foreign key on any `department` column. information_schema.columns confirms `department` is `text` on all seven tables that carry it (rota_shifts, rota_shift_templates, rota_published_shifts, rota_shift_rejections, rota_shift_calendar_cancellations, department_budgets, employee_reliability_events).

6) 'Per-shift, not per-employee' CONFIRMED two ways: `employees` has no `department` column at all (absent from the information_schema sweep), and 9 employees have worked more than one distinct department (`GROUP BY employee_id HAVING count(DISTINCT department) > 1`).

ADDITIONAL FINDING that strengthens the claim's own recommendation: `SELECT count(*) FROM rota_shifts WHERE department NOT IN (SELECT name FROM departments)` returns 0. Every live shift department is already a valid departments row, so an FK could be added today with zero data cleanup and zero violating rows — the FK route is cheaper than the claim asserts.
verdict.implication: A bar/kitchen-only tag taxonomy is factually wrong against the live rota. Since the rota is the stated source of truth for open/close responsibility and shift-linked task assignment, a two-value tag silently drops 126 shifts (~8% of all 1516) covering runner, cleaning and host — including the 18 'cleaning' shifts, which are the shifts most likely to carry exactly the open/close checklist tasks this feature is being built for. Because department is free TEXT with no constraint anywhere, nothing in the database will reject a bad tag value or warn at write time; a mismatch surfaces only as tasks never appearing for those staff. The FK-to-departments route is validated: departments.name is a PRIMARY KEY, 0 rows violate it today, and it means adding a 6th department later needs no migration and no code change to the checklists tags.
verdict.blocker: YES — SURVIVES. Owner must decide before schema work: (a) checklist task tags FK to departments.name (RECOMMENDED — departments.name is already a PK, zero rows violate the FK today so it applies with no cleanup, and future departments need no migration), or (b) a separate bar/kitchen-only taxonomy with an explicit documented mapping for runner/host/cleaning. Option (b) requires the owner to state where those 126 shifts' tasks go; leaving it implicit means those staff silently see no checklist. Note the owner's brief says 'tagged BAR or KITCHEN', so this contradicts a stated decision and cannot be resolved by inference.

verified.claim: Publish horizon and the unpublished-week fallback

verdict.topic: Publish horizon, the three rota_weeks states, and the unmeasurability of publish lag
verdict.detail:
CONFIRMED (with one addition the claim understates). Verified against live prod Supabase (project tfcasgxopxegwrabvwat, rota_weeks joined to rota_shifts + rota_published_shifts) and src/app/actions/rota.ts.

1. PUBLISH HORIZON — CONFIRMED exactly. Last published week_start is 2026-08-10 (covers to 2026-08-16). From today 2026-07-17 that is 30 days / ~4 weeks ahead. Every week_start from 2026-05-04 through 2026-08-10 is status='published'.

2. THREE DISTINCT STATES — CONFIRMED exactly, numbers match to the shift.
   - published-with-shifts: 2026-05-04 … 2026-08-10
   - draft-with-shifts: 2026-08-17 (23 draft shifts, 0 published) and 2026-08-24 (23 draft shifts, 0 published) — 23 EACH, as claimed
   - draft-empty: 2026-08-31, 09-07, 09-14, 09-21, 09-28 — all status='draft', published_at=null, ZERO shifts
   So the two-state mental model (published vs not) is genuinely wrong; there are three.

3. published_at IS OVERWRITTEN ON EVERY RE-PUBLISH — CONFIRMED at src/app/actions/rota.ts:3009-3014. The update is unconditional, with no COALESCE / 'only if null' guard:
     .update({ status: 'published', published_at: new Date().toISOString(), published_by: user?.id, has_unpublished_changes: false })
   Every re-publish stamps 'now'. All three cited examples verify: 2026-06-08 → published_at 2026-06-13; 2026-05-11 → 2026-05-21; 2026-07-06 → 2026-07-09. All are AFTER their own week_start, which is impossible as a first-publish date. So published_at is a last-touch timestamp and historical publish lag is NOT derivable from this column.
   ADDITIONAL EVIDENCE the claim did not cite, pointing the same way: 2026-06-22 shows published_at 2026-06-01 and 2026-06-29 shows 2026-06-16 — i.e. BEFORE their week_start. The column is scattered in both directions, reinforcing that it is noise, not signal.

4. has_unpublished_changes=true FOR 2026-07-27 — CONFIRMED (published_at 2026-07-09, 23 draft shifts vs 24 published shifts — the draft has genuinely drifted from the snapshot). INCOMPLETE, not wrong: two other weeks also carry the flag, 2026-05-04 (24 draft / 21 published) and 2026-06-15 (21 draft / 22 published). Both are in the past, so the claim's substantive point — that drift exists mid-horizon — stands. The flag is set from at least seven call sites (rota.ts:1242, 1359, 1463, 1832, 2439, 2550, 2681).

5. ADDITION THE CLAIM MISSES — draft/published shift counts diverge on several weeks that are NOT flagged has_unpublished_changes: 2026-07-13 (25 draft / 22 published, flag=false), 2026-07-06 (23 / 22, flag=false), 2026-05-18 (27 / 26, flag=false). So has_unpublished_changes is not a reliable indicator of drift either. Any checklist code that resolves 'who was rostered' must read rota_published_shifts directly rather than trusting the flag or the status alone.
verdict.implication: The checklist's rota lookup must branch on three states, not two, and must read rota_published_shifts (the snapshot staff actually saw) as the accountability source — never rota_shifts. Concretely: for a shift date inside the horizon, published shifts exist and attribution works; for 2026-08-17/24 a draft exists with 23 shifts but staff have seen nothing, so attributing to it would blame people for a plan never shown to them; for 2026-08-31 onward there is no plan at all. Because published_at is last-touch and has_unpublished_changes is unreliable (point 5), neither can be used to reason about what staff saw or when — only the presence of rota_published_shifts rows for the week can. Also do not build any 'typical publish lag' heuristic (e.g. auto-generating checklists N days ahead based on observed lead time) from published_at; the data cannot support it. Practically the horizon is comfortable — 30 days of published rota is far more than a daily/weekly checklist needs — so the fallback is an edge case, not the common path.
verdict.blocker: YES (narrow, unchanged — the blocker survives). Owner must accept: when no rota is published for a shift date, the checklist STILL APPEARS but nobody is pre-assigned or pre-blamed — the open/close list runs unattributed until someone ticks their name from the clocked-in list. The alternative, falling back to rota_shifts drafts, would rest accountability on a plan staff never saw (proven live: 2026-08-17 and 2026-08-24 each hold 23 draft shifts with zero published rows). Recommend the former. Second, smaller decision implied by finding 5: attribution must read rota_published_shifts rather than rota_shifts even INSIDE the horizon, because draft and published counts already diverge on live weeks (2026-07-13: 25 vs 22) without the drift flag being set.

verified.claim: Timeclock attribution is viable but the clocked-in list is thin and sometimes empty

verdict.topic: Timeclock attribution viability — timeclock_sessions data density and 'currently clocked in' definition
verdict.detail: CONFIRMED (one figure understated in the claim's favour). Every datum reproduces against the live prod DB (project tfcasgxopxegwrabvwat). (1) Volume/shape: `select count(*), count(distinct employee_id), min/max(work_date), count(*) filter (where work_date >= '2026-07-01'), count(*) filter (where clock_out_at is null)` returns exactly total=1222, emps=23, 2024-12-20..2026-07-16, since_jul=33, open_now=0. (2) Columns: information_schema.columns for timeclock_sessions returns exactly the 21 columns listed in the claim, in the stated order — no additions, no omissions. (3) 'Currently clocked in' = clock_out_at IS NULL is the codebase's own definition, not an inference: src/app/actions/timeclock.ts:200 uses `.is('clock_out_at', null)` as the open-session test (also :274, :325), and src/app/api/cron/rota-auto-close/route.ts:41 selects `.is('clock_out_at', null)` as the set needing closure. (4) Live daily clocked-vs-rostered (timeclock_sessions.work_date vs rota_shifts.shift_date, 2026-07-01..07-16) matches the claim's spot examples precisely: 07-11 = 2 clocked vs 4 rostered; 07-05 = 4 vs 6; 07-06 = 2 clocked vs 1 rostered (unscheduled cover, the only day clocked > rostered). Range is 1–4 clocked against 1–6 rostered. CORRECTION, understated: clocked < rostered on 13 of 16 days, not 10 — only 07-06 (2>1), 07-13 (1=1) and 07-14 (3=3) are not short. The thinness the claim describes is worse than it says, which strengthens rather than weakens its conclusion. (5) Data is clean, not an artefact of auto-closure: only 1 of 458 sessions since 2026-01-01 is is_auto_close (0.2%), so the low headcounts are real behaviour, not cron noise. NOT REFUTED on any point.
verdict.implication: The clocked-in list is a real, live, correctly-defined signal, but it is too sparse to be the sole attribution source — on 13 of the last 16 days it would have offered fewer names than were actually rostered, and at query time it offered zero. The claim's recommended picker (clocked-in first/prominent, then rostered-but-not-clocked-in, then search-all fallback) is the right shape and is required, not optional: a clocked-in-only picker would routinely present an empty or incomplete list on the FOH modal. Two implementation notes the claim does not cover: (a) filter the picker on work_date as well as `clock_out_at IS NULL` — rota-auto-close runs at 05:00 daily (vercel.json:209-210), so between midnight and 05:00 a previous day's unclosed session would otherwise surface as 'currently clocked in' (0 such rows right now, but the pub closes late and the window is real); (b) clock-in is PIN-gated (src/app/actions/timeclock.ts:193), which does not conflict with the no-PIN checklist attribution decision — the checklist reads sessions, it does not create them.

verified.claim: Employee audit log target for spot checks

verdict.topic: Employee audit log target for spot checks
verdict.detail:
CONFIRMED on facts, PARTIAL on framing. Live prod (project tfcasgxopxegwrabvwat) information_schema confirms the audit tables are exactly: audit_logs, booking_audit, invoice_audit, private_booking_audit. No employee_audit / employee_audit_log table exists, and no spot_check* table exists (grep over src + supabase returns zero hits). The claim's table list is accurate.

Two material corrections the claim misses:

(1) The 'employee audit log' the owner refers to ALREADY EXISTS as a surface, and it is a filtered view of audit_logs, not a table. src/services/employees.ts:1019 fetches it as `supabase.from('audit_logs').select('*').eq('resource_type','employee').eq('resource_id', employeeId).order('created_at',{ascending:false}).limit(50)`, rendered by EmployeeAuditTrail (imported at src/app/(authenticated)/employees/[employee_id]/page.tsx:22, rendered at :447 under `<section id="audit">`). Prod audit_logs holds 2,212 rows at resource_type='employee'. There is also a prod VIEW employee_version_history built over `audit_logs WHERE resource_type='employee' AND operation_status='success'` (pg_get_viewdef confirmed).

(2) The claim's recommended shape is already the established house pattern, so it is not a novel design decision. Prod table employee_reliability_events has precisely the recommended columns: employee_id, event_type, event_at, source/source_table/source_id, idempotency_key, shift_id, shift_date, department, score_eligible (boolean), note, metadata. That is a dedicated per-employee, per-shift, explicitly-scored events table — the exact analogue of the proposed spot_checks table, including the scoring-eligibility flag.
verdict.implication: Build a dedicated spot_checks table modelled directly on employee_reliability_events (employee_id, checker_id, task_instance_id, passed, note, checked_at, score_eligible, idempotency_key) — this is a proven in-repo pattern, not a new invention, which lowers the design risk the claim implies. Then add a logAuditEvent() call for the trail. Two hard constraints follow from the existing surface: (a) for a spot check to show on the employee record at all, the audit_logs row MUST use resource_type='employee' with resource_id = the EMPLOYEE's id — any other resource_type (e.g. 'spot_check') will be invisible to both EmployeeAuditTrail and the employee_version_history view; (b) that query is hard-capped at limit 50 in src/services/employees.ts:1019, so ~2 spot checks/day would flush an employee's HR change history off the panel within about a month. If spot checks are routed through audit_logs, that limit must be raised or the query split by operation/resource type. This also independently vindicates the claim's warning against overloading audit_logs alone: employee_version_history assigns version_number via row_number() over ALL employee audit rows, so spot-check rows would silently corrupt the version numbering that the migration's diff functions (supabase/migrations/20251123120000_squashed.sql:281-286, :1236) rely on.
verdict.blocker: YES (small, and narrower than stated) — the owner/architect must confirm the dual-write: dedicated spot_checks table for the domain data and scoring, PLUS a logAuditEvent() row using resource_type='employee' and resource_id=<employee_id> so it lands on the existing EmployeeAuditTrail. Two follow-on decisions are forced and should be made at the same time: (1) raise or restructure the limit 50 in src/services/employees.ts:1019 so spot checks do not evict HR history; (2) exclude spot-check rows from the employee_version_history view's row_number() version numbering, or accept that version numbers shift. Do not route per-person scoring through audit_logs alone.

verified.claim: business_hours shape — every day closes 22:00, and close-shift ends coincide exactly

verdict.detail:
PARTIAL — first half CONFIRMED verbatim, second half REFUTED.

CONFIRMED (live query, project tfcasgxopxegwrabvwat, business_hours all 7 rows): every day is_closed=false and closes=22:00:00. Sun opens 12:00 (kitchen 13:00–18:00, schedule_config sunday_lunch slot 13:00–18:00 capacity 50); Mon 16:00–22:00 with is_kitchen_closed=TRUE and kitchen_opens/kitchen_closes NULL; Tue/Wed/Thu 16:00–22:00 (kitchen 16:00–21:00); Fri 16:00–22:00 (kitchen 16:00–21:00) with schedule_config lunch slot 12:00–14:30 — genuinely inconsistent with opens=16:00; Sat 12:00–22:00 (kitchen 12:00–19:00, schedule_config dinner slot ends 21:00). All 7 rows updated_at=2026-03-22 15:06:39+00. Column list matches exactly (id, day_of_week, opens, closes, kitchen_opens, kitchen_closes, is_closed, created_at, updated_at, is_kitchen_closed, schedule_config jsonb). Validation confirmed at src/services/business-hours.ts:29 (businessHoursSchema), timeSchema at :8, 'Opening time is required when the venue is open' at :45, 'Closing time is required when the venue is open' at :53, invoked via safeParse at :279.

REFUTED — 'close-shift ends coincide exactly' is false. Over 2026-04-01→2026-07-17, excluding 00:00–00:00 placeholder rows, the latest real shift end per day equals 22:00 on only 98 of 108 days. On 4 Fridays and 6 Saturdays the last shift ends 00:00:00 — two hours AFTER business_hours.closes=22:00. These are precisely the close shifts: rota_shifts.name='Friday Close' (19:00–00:00) and 'Saturday Close' (18:00–00:00, also 20:00–00:00), plus a 'Host' 22:00–00:00. So rota and business_hours diverge exactly on the shifts the close checklist attaches to.

This inverts the claim's reasoning. Tue–Thu/Sun coincide, so a spot-check DOES look plausible — but the divergence is real on Fri/Sat, which means 'rota as source of truth' is CORRECT and necessary, not a trap. business_hours is the thing that is wrong in production: it says 22:00 on nights the pub demonstrably trades to midnight.
verdict.implication: Do not derive open/close windows from business_hours — it understates Fri/Sat by 2h and its Fri schedule_config lunch slot (12:00–14:30) contradicts opens=16:00. The owner's 'rota is source of truth' decision is vindicated by the data. But deriving the close window from rota_shifts is NOT a simple MAX(end_time): a real blocker surfaced below.
verdict.blocker: YES — a real blocker survives, different from the one stated. rota_shifts.end_time='00:00:00' is overloaded and cannot be disambiguated by the obvious fields. (1) It means genuine midnight for 'Friday Close'/'Saturday Close' shifts, AND it is the placeholder for 20 sick rows (status='sick', name="Couldn't Work", start_time=00:00:00, end_time=00:00:00) — matching the known convention that absences are rota_shifts rows, not leave_requests. A naive MAX(end_time) sorts midnight closes to the EARLIEST end; the naive fix (map 00:00→1440) silently promotes every sick placeholder into a midnight close shift. (2) is_overnight does not save you: identical 'Saturday Close' 18:00–00:00 rows are split 4×is_overnight=false vs 3×is_overnight=true, and 'Friday Close' 19:00–00:00 is split 3×true vs 1×false — the flag is inconsistently populated for the same shift. The checklists build must define an explicit rule for close-window derivation (filter status<>'sick' AND NOT (start_time=00:00 AND end_time=00:00), treat end_time=00:00 with start_time<>00:00 as next-day midnight, and ignore is_overnight) before 'the person closing is responsible for all checks' can be computed correctly. Otherwise close checklists mis-fire on Fri/Sat — the two busiest nights — and credit or blame the wrong person.

verified.claim: audit_logs IS queryable per-employee, but only by string convention

verdict.topic: audit_logs per-employee queryability and the 50-row timeline cap
verdict.detail: CONFIRMED — every stated element verified against the code. (1) Table definition matches exactly at supabase/migrations/20251123120000_squashed.sql:1746-1762: id, created_at, user_id, user_email, operation_type, resource_type, resource_id TEXT, operation_status with CHECK ('success'|'failure'), ip_address, user_agent, old_values, new_values, error_message, additional_info jsonb. (2) NO FK to employees — the only FK on the table is audit_logs_user_id_fkey FOREIGN KEY (user_id) REFERENCES auth.users(id) at squashed.sql:4436, which also confirms user_id is the ACTOR's auth user, not the subject employee. Grepping all migrations for audit_logs REFERENCES/FOREIGN KEY/ADD COLUMN returns only that one line; the three later migrations touching audit_logs (20260608000000_d19_audit_booking_hold_status.sql, 20260702000000_log_error_fixes.sql, 20260706000000_employee_reliability_events.sql) add no FK or column. (3) The per-employee fetch is verbatim as claimed at src/services/employees.ts:1019 — `.from('audit_logs').select('*').eq('resource_type','employee').eq('resource_id', employeeId).order('created_at', {ascending:false}).limit(50)`. I attempted three refutations, all failed: (a) 'string convention' might be unsupported/unindexed — refuted, the convention is deliberately indexed: idx_audit_logs_employee_history on (resource_id, created_at DESC) WHERE resource_type='employee' AND operation_status='success' (squashed.sql:3842), plus idx_audit_logs_resource (3850), idx_audit_logs_resource_composite (3854), idx_audit_logs_resource_created (3858). So it is genuinely queryable, by convention, on purpose. (b) resource_id TEXT vs uuid employeeId might break the eq — refuted, PostgREST casts fine and 20+ write sites populate resource_type:'employee' with the employee id (src/app/actions/employeeActions.ts:336,357,427,...,1233; src/app/actions/rota.ts:1746). (c) The UI might paginate around the 50-cap — refuted, and this makes the blocker WORSE than stated: src/services/employees.ts:1042 returns auditLogs straight to src/app/(authenticated)/employees/[employee_id]/page.tsx:103,450, into src/components/features/employees/EmployeeAuditTrail.tsx:26-45, which merges audit rows with notes into one flat sorted timeline with NO pagination, NO 'load more', and NO type filter anywhere in its 364 lines. The 50 is a hard server-side cap with no client escape hatch, and it is applied BEFORE the merge with notes, so 50 audit rows compete with notes for the same view.
verdict.implication: Spot checks CAN be tracked on the employee audit log with no schema change — write resource_type:'employee', resource_id:<employeeId>, and the existing partial index already serves the query. But at 2 spot checks/day (~730/year, per the owner's decision), spot-check rows would flush hires, RTW checks, pay changes and every other employee event out of the 50-row window within roughly 25 days, destroying the timeline the owner relies on. Note the build already has a purpose-built alternative: employee_reliability_events (supabase/migrations/20260706000000_employee_reliability_events.sql:3-5) is a real per-employee table with a proper FK — employee_id UUID NOT NULL REFERENCES public.employees(employee_id) ON DELETE CASCADE — and an index on (employee_id, event_at DESC) at line 45. So the owner's 'must also be tracked on the EMPLOYEE AUDIT LOG' requirement is satisfiable either by writing spot checks to audit_logs (and fixing the cap) or by a dedicated spot-check table surfaced into the same timeline.
verdict.blocker: Decision needed, and it survives with a sharper edge than stated: if spot checks are written to audit_logs, the hard 50-row cap at src/services/employees.ts:1019 must be raised or made filterable by operation_type/resource sub-type, AND EmployeeAuditTrail.tsx must gain pagination or a filter — it currently has none, so raising the limit alone just makes one unbounded scrolling list. The cheaper option is to keep high-frequency spot checks out of audit_logs (e.g. a dedicated table alongside employee_reliability_events) and merge them into the timeline view at read time. Either path needs the owner's call before the checklists schema is fixed.

verified.claim: employee_reliability_events is the best-fitting existing home pattern

verdict.topic: employee_reliability_events as the home pattern for checklists
verdict.detail:
PARTIAL — every descriptive fact in the claim is CONFIRMED, but the 'best-fitting home' framing and the enum-extension option are REFUTED.

CONFIRMED verbatim: supabase/migrations/20260706000000_employee_reliability_events.sql:3-41 defines the table exactly as described (employee_id UUID NOT NULL REFERENCES employees(employee_id) ON DELETE CASCADE at :4, event_type CHECK enum of exactly 9 rota/leave values at :6-19, event_at/source/source_table/source_id, idempotency_key TEXT NOT NULL UNIQUE at :25, shift_id, shift_date, department, score_eligible BOOLEAN NOT NULL DEFAULT TRUE at :39, note TEXT, metadata JSONB). RLS enabled at :58; the ONLY policy is SELECT to `employees:view` holders (:62-68) — no INSERT/UPDATE policy for authenticated, confirmed. Writes go via service role: src/services/employee-reliability.ts:147 recordReliabilityEvent, :164 `createAdminClient()`, :194-196 `.upsert(payload, { onConflict: 'idempotency_key', ignoreDuplicates: true })`. Dedicated tab confirmed at src/app/(authenticated)/employees/[employee_id]/page.tsx:278-279 ('Reliability' → EmployeeReliabilityTab), fed by getEmployeeReliabilityData (imported :36, called :79). Double-write audit trail confirmed at src/services/employee-reliability.ts:207 recordEmployeeReliabilityAudit → audit_logs with resource_type 'employee'. This migration is the ONLY one referencing the table (grep across supabase/migrations/), so no later ALTER relaxes the enum or adds a write policy.

REFUTED — the enum-extension half of the decision is not viable, so 'best-fitting home' overstates the fit:
1. Scoring is a CLOSED system, not a generic event log. src/lib/employee-reliability-scoring.ts:4-16 hardcodes RELIABILITY_EVENT_TYPES as a TS literal union mirroring the DB CHECK; :128-178 is an exhaustive `switch (event.event_type)` over those 9 cases with no default branch; :232-234 hardcodes display labels per type. A 10th enum value would flow through the switch and produce nothing.
2. The score denominator is rota-specific. :79-82 SHIFT_DECISION_EVENTS and :179-180 `acceptance = 45 * (acceptanceCredit / shiftDecisionCount)` — the score is an acceptance/disruption model over shift decisions (:186-190 penalties for couldntWork/lateRejectionAttempts/rejections). Checklist ticks have no acceptance semantics; they would have to be written with score_eligible=false, meaning the reuse buys the table's shape but none of its scoring.
3. Single-employee FK conflicts with the owner's attribution decision. employee_id is a single NOT NULL FK (:4), but a checklist tick may credit MULTIPLE clocked-in staff. That is a 1:N join, structurally impossible in this table without one row per person and a synthetic idempotency_key.
verdict.implication: Reuse the table's PATTERN, not the table. employee_reliability_events is a sound template to copy — service-role-only writes + SELECT-gated RLS + idempotency_key UNIQUE upsert + score_eligible flag + metadata JSONB + double-write to audit_logs — and that pattern maps well onto the checklists build (the audit_logs double-write at src/services/employee-reliability.ts:207 is exactly the mechanism the owner's 'spot checks must hit the EMPLOYEE AUDIT LOG' requirement needs, with resource_type 'employee'). But checklist completions need their own tables: a completion row (task, shift, value, notes, spot-check result) plus a join table for the multi-person credit, because the single NOT NULL employee_id cannot express 'credit these three people'. Per-person checklist scoring must be its own calculator — calculateBusinessReliabilityScore cannot be extended, only paralleled.
verdict.blocker: The stated blocker does not survive as a decision — the sibling table is forced, not chosen. Extending the event_type enum is refuted by three independent constraints (closed switch at src/lib/employee-reliability-scoring.ts:128-178 with no default, a shift-acceptance denominator at :179-180 that checklist ticks cannot populate, and a single NOT NULL employee_id at migration :4 that cannot hold the owner's multi-person attribution). No owner decision is required here; proceed with sibling tables copying the pattern. The real open question the spec should raise instead is whether the checklist per-person score surfaces as a second employee tab alongside 'Reliability' (page.tsx:278-279) or only on the superadmin insights screen — the owner said superadmin-visible with no consequences, which suggests superadmin screen only, but the existing Reliability tab is visible to any employees:view holder, so putting it on the employee record would widen the audience beyond what was decided.

verified.claim: Employee version history exists as a separate view

verdict.detail: CONFIRMED (with corrections). The view exists at supabase/migrations/20251123120000_squashed.sql:2225 (claim's filename 'squallshed.sql' is wrong; the file is 20251123120000_squashed.sql), built from audit_logs (al.id, al.created_at, al.user_id, al.user_email, al.operation_type, al.resource_id AS employee_id, old_values, new_values, ip_address) — matching the claimed column list. Actions confirmed at src/app/actions/employee-history.ts:12-55 (getEmployeeChangesSummary → RPC get_employee_changes_summary, gated on user_has_permission(employees,'view') at :23-31) and a restoreEmployeeVersion path at :58. Component confirmed at src/components/features/employees/EmployeeRecentChanges.tsx:44, mounted via src/app/(authenticated)/employees/[employee_id]/page.tsx:23,458. THE CLAIM'S OPEN QUESTION ('filter by operation_type?') RESOLVES TO NO: the view's only WHERE is squashed.sql:2246 `al.resource_type = 'employee' AND al.operation_status = 'success'`, and the effective get_employee_changes_summary redefinition (squashed.sql:14937, superseding the earlier :753 definition) filters only on squashed.sql:15020-15023 `al.resource_type='employee' AND al.resource_id=p_employee_id::text AND al.created_at BETWEEN … AND al.operation_status='success'`. Neither filters operation_type. Two corrections to the claim's framing: (a) restoreEmployeeVersion (:58) and compareEmployeeVersions (:84) lack the `export` keyword and have no importers — only getEmployeeChangesSummary is live; (b) squashed.sql:2236 defines version_number as row_number() OVER (PARTITION BY al.resource_id ORDER BY al.created_at), a positional count over every successful employee audit row.
verdict.implication: Adding a new operation_type to audit_logs against resource_type='employee' for checklist spot checks has two effects. (1) Pollution: both the view and the summary RPC select all operation types unconditionally, so spot-check rows flow straight into the employee's Recent Changes panel. It will not crash — squashed.sql:15009 `ELSE ARRAY[]::text[]` and :15017 `ELSE operation_type` handle unknown types — but rows render as a raw slug and consume slots in the top-5 .slice(0,5) at EmployeeRecentChanges.tsx:54. (2) Version renumbering: because version_number is a row_number() over all employee audit rows, new inserts shift the version numbers of existing history, silently changing what restore_employee_version (squashed.sql:15037) and compare_employee_versions (squashed.sql:14890) resolve to. This is latent, not live, since both callers are unexported. The clean route for spot-check tracking is a distinct resource_type (e.g. 'employee_spot_check') rather than a new operation_type under resource_type='employee' — that sidesteps both problems with no change to the view or the RPC.
verdict.blocker: Blocker survives, and is sharper than stated: there is NO operation_type filter to rely on. Any new operation_type written against resource_type='employee' will (a) appear unfiltered in the employee Recent Changes panel and (b) shift version_number for all existing employee history rows, changing what restore_employee_version and compare_employee_versions resolve to. Decide before building spot-check audit writes: use a distinct resource_type (recommended — zero impact on the view/RPC), or accept these effects and add an operation_type filter to both squashed.sql:2246 and :15020.

verified.claim: KILLER FINDING: the person who closes never clocks in — attribution by clocked-in will be empty for closing/kitchen tasks

verdict.topic: Attribution via clocked-in staff — 'the closer never clocks in'
verdict.detail: PARTIAL — the headline is REFUTED, a narrower kitchen-only version is CONFIRMED. Live prod (project tfcasgxopxegwrabvwat), rota_shifts vs timeclock_sessions on employee_id+date, shift_date in [current_date-30, current_date]: 101 shifts total (97 with an employee), 32 with no timeclock session = 33%, not 179/246 = 73%. I could not reproduce 246 shifts in any 30-day window (90 days only gives 306). Per person the numbers are also wrong: Billy Summers 18 shifts (not 46), Peter Pitcher 3 (not 9), Laura Bradshaw 12 (not 27), Lance Marlow 12 (not 27), Amanda Jones 25 (not 58), Ryan Bond 6 (not 20), Marty Pitcher-Summers 5 (not 13). Crucially, by department: bar 59 shifts / 7 with no session (88% do clock in), and of the 29 bar shifts ending 21:30 or later — i.e. the closers — only 1 had no session. Recent clock-outs run 22:00–22:39 (and one 01:25), so the closer is normally still clocked in when closing checks happen. What IS true: kitchen 27 shifts / 19 no session (70%), and Billy Summers 18/18 kitchen shifts with no session (0% clocked in), plus cleaning 4/4 and host 1/1. 'Right now there are 0 open sessions' is real (SELECT count(*) FROM timeclock_sessions WHERE clock_out_at IS NULL = 0) but proves nothing — it is daytime and the pub is shut; sessions are open during trading. Schema confirmed: rota_shifts(employee_id, shift_date, department, start_time, end_time, status), timeclock_sessions(employee_id, work_date, clock_in_at, clock_out_at, linked_shift_id) — see supabase/migrations/20260228100000_rota_system.sql.
verdict.implication: A clocked-in picker is a workable default for closing and mid-shift BAR tasks — the closer is clocked in ~97% of the time. It is NOT workable for KITCHEN tasks: Billy, who chooses the spot checks and works every kitchen shift, has never clocked in once in 30 days, so a kitchen fridge/cellar-temp modal would offer an empty or wrong name list. Fix is local, not architectural: seed the picker from clocked-in staff, fall back to rota_shifts for the work_date filtered by department, and allow free selection of any Active employee.
verdict.blocker: Owner decision, but much smaller than stated: confirm the attribution picker shows clocked-in staff first, plus anyone rota'd on that date for the task's department (bar/kitchen), plus free selection of any Active employee. This is needed for kitchen tasks only — bar/closing attribution works from timeclock alone.
